This double-phase appears again in 8:1–3 when Ephraim complains about being left out of the main battle.
7.24: "Come down against the Midianites and hold the waters" — lipdū ʾet-hammayim (seize the waters): cutting off retreat by controlling the water crossings, a standard ANE military tactic. Ephraim's role in the pursuit (capturing Oreb and Zeeb, v25) is theologically significant — the tribe that would later complain (8:1) was actually given the decisive honor of capturing the princes.
7.25: Oreb ("Raven") and Zeeb ("Wolf") — the names carry symbolic weight: predatory animals as names for enemy leaders. Their execution locations — ṣūr Ōrēb (Rock of Oreb) and yeqeb Zēʾēb (Winepress of Zeeb) — become permanent landmarks referenced in Ps 83:11 and Isa 10:26, where they become the paradigm of YHWH's defeat of Israel's enemies. The heads brought to Gideon "beyond the Jordan" confirm the completion of the rout.

8.1: "What is this that you have done to us, not to call us when you went to fight?" — tribal honor in the ANE required being summoned to significant battles; exclusion was an insult (ḥerpāh, reproach/shame, is the implied register). Ephraim was the dominant tribe in central Canaan and expected primacy. Their complaint is fierce (wayyārîbūn ʾittô bᵊḥāzāqāh, they quarreled with him sharply) — the verb rîb is the word for legal disputation and covenant controversy.
8.2: "Is not the gleaning of Ephraim better than the vintage of Abiezer?" — a proverb-style argument. ʿōlelôt ʾEprayim (the gleanings of Ephraim) vs. bᵊṣîr ʾăbîʿezer (the vintage of Abiezer/Gideon's clan). Gleaning was what was left after harvest; vintage was the main crop. Gideon inverts the normal valuation: Ephraim's secondary role (capturing the princes) was worth more than his own primary campaign. Diplomatic self-deprecation that deflects the tribal grievance.
8.3: "What was I able to do compared to you? Then their anger subsided" — wā'āpēr rûḥām mēʿālāyw: their spirit relaxed/was assuaged toward him. The root rāpāh (relax, be slack) describes the deflation of anger. Gideon's rhetorical skill succeeds where force would have inflamed. The episode contrasts with Jephthah's identical conflict with Ephraim (12:1–6), where refusal to apply diplomatic wisdom leads to civil war.
8.4: "Exhausted yet still pursuing" — ʿāyēp wᵊhēm rōdᵊpîm: faint/exhausted (ʿāyēp) while (wᵊhēm, "yet they are") pursuing. The participle rōdᵊpîm marks ongoing action: they have not stopped despite the physical cost. The juxtaposition — weakness and persistence — echoes the "faint yet pursuing" spirituality of Isaiah 40:31 ("those who hope in YHWH will renew their strength").
8.5: "I am pursuing Zebah and Zalmunna, the kings of Midian" — the two kings' names: Zebaḥ ("sacrifice/slaughter") and Ṣalmunnāʾ ("image withheld" or "shadow of protection denied"). Gideon makes his case to Succoth on the basis of ongoing holy war: the pursuit of the kings is not yet complete and feeding the troops is an act of covenant solidarity.
8.6: "Are the hands of Zebah and Zalmunna already in your hand?" — hakap Zebaḥ wᵊṢalmunnāʾ ʿattāh bᵊyādekā: an ironic rhetorical question. "Hand" (yād) appears twice: "hands of the kings" vs. "in your hand." The officials of Succoth demand proof of victory before committing provisions — a calculus of self-interest over covenant solidarity. They are Israelites who refuse to help fellow Israelites.
8.7: "I will flail your flesh with the thorns of the wilderness and with briers" — dōš ʾet-bᵊśarkem: the verb dāš means to thresh (as grain is threshed); here applied to human bodies. The punishment fits the refusal: those who withheld bread from weary fighters will themselves be threshed. The specific mention of qōṣê hammidbār (thorns of the wilderness) connects the punishment to the very landscape Gideon traversed.
8.8: Penuel answered him just as Succoth had — the doubling of the refusal (two towns, same response) constitutes a legal pattern in ANE contexts: a sin established by two witnesses. It also marks the theological collapse of Transjordanian Israel's solidarity with YHWH's ongoing war — two communities choosing self-preservation over covenant loyalty.
8.9: "When I return in peace, I will break down this tower" — Gideon's announcement creates a narrative promise that the reader knows will be fulfilled (8:17). The tower (migdāl) was the central fortification of Penuel — its destruction would be a permanent public humiliation. The word "in peace" (bᵊšālôm) is ironic: Gideon plans violent retribution framed as the completion of peaceful return.
8.10: "About fifteen thousand men — all who were left" — the narrator provides the arithmetic: 120,000 swords had fallen; 15,000 remained. A 13:1 kill ratio in the battle. The number 120,000 is conventionally large in ANE military narratives; the 15,000 is the routed remnant. The geographical note (Karkor) places the final engagement deep in Transjordanian territory, far east of Israel's settled land.
8.11: "The army was off guard" — kî hammaḥăneh hāyāh beṭaḥ: the camp was trusting/secure (beṭaḥ, the same word used for a city dwelling in safety). The element of surprise — Gideon goes by the route of the tent-dwellers (nomadic path) that the Midianites did not expect to be used — is the final military stroke. The defeat of Zebah and Zalmunna mirrors the defeat of Oreb and Zeeb: YHWH's holy war is complete.
8.12: "He pursued them and captured the two kings" — wayyirdōp wayyilkōd ʾet-šᵊnê malkê Midyān: pursue and capture (both in perfect consecutive, marking completed action). "Throwing the whole army into a panic" (wayyaḥrad kol-hammaḥăneh, the whole camp trembled/was thrown into confusion) — the same divine panic (ḥarrādāh) that YHWH sent earlier on Gideon's enemies.
8.13: Gideon returned "from battle by the ascent of Heres" — a geographical note fixing the precise return route. Ḥeres may mean "sun" — a possible solar toponym. The brief notice of return after battle is a standard ANE victory formula: the campaign is complete, the king/judge returns victorious.
8.14: "He captured a young man of Succoth and questioned him. The young man wrote down for him…seventy-seven men" — one of the earliest references to writing (wayyiktōb, and he wrote) by a private individual in Judges. That a "young man" (naʿar) could write the names of 77 civic leaders suggests a surprising level of literacy in pre-monarchic Transjordanian Israel. The seventy-seven matches a conventional number in Ugaritic texts for divine or royal councils.
8.15: "Behold Zebah and Zalmunna, about whom you taunted me" — the word "taunted" is ḥērap (to reproach/defame), the strongest word for public shaming. Gideon keeps his promise: the proof of victory is presented to the very men who demanded it. The legal procedure of confronting accusers with evidence is precise.
8.16: "He taught the men of Succoth a lesson" — wayyōdaʿ ʾet-ʾanšê Sūkkôt: literally "he made the men of Succoth to know." The Hebrew yādaʿ (to know) in a causative form — to teach someone to know through painful experience. The thorns and briers used for threshing flesh are literally what Gideon had promised in v7.
8.17: He tore down the tower of Penuel and killed the men of the city — the destruction is exactly as promised in v9. No additional narrative comment is made; the action speaks. The killing of the men of Penuel is more severe than the punishment of Succoth (thorns). Penuel had a strategic tower and may have been a more fortified town; its refusal to help was a more deliberate act of defiance.
8.18: "They were like you — every one of them had the bearing of a king's son" — kᵊtō'ar bᵊnê hammelek (like the appearance of the sons of a king). The enemy kings' observation identifies Gideon's brothers as men of noble bearing. The revelation — Gideon's brothers were killed at Tabor — transforms the military execution into a blood-vengeance obligation (gō'ēl haddām). What was a holy war now also carries a personal dimension.
8.19: "As the LORD lives, if you had let them live, I would not kill you" — the oath formula ḥay YHWH (as YHWH lives) binds Gideon's statement. The conditional confirms that the execution of Zebah and Zalmunna is blood-vengeance for the murder of his full brothers (bᵊnê ʾimmî, sons of my mother). The same-mother qualification marks them as full brothers vs. half-brothers — the distinction matters in ANE inheritance and honor law.
8.20: "Rise and kill them!" But the young man did not draw his sword, for he was afraid — Jether, as firstborn son, would gain honor by executing the kings. His hesitation (yārēʾ kî ʿôd naʿar, he was afraid because he was still a youth) is a narrative foil: the young man is unwilling to take the honor. Zebah and Zalmunna's response in v21 shows they understand the honor system — they prefer to die by Gideon's hand, not a boy's.
8.21: "For as the man is, so is his strength" — Gideon rose and killed them — the proverb (kî kā'îš gᵊbūrātô: as the man, so is his strength) is the enemy kings' appeal to warrior honor: they want to die at the hand of a worthy opponent. Gideon takes the śahᵃrōnîm (crescent ornaments — lunar amulets) from their camels' necks — cultic trophies that become the material for the ill-fated ephod (v27).
8.22: "Rule over us — you and your son and your grandson" — the offer of hereditary kingship (ûbinkā ûben-binkā) makes this a formal dynastic proposal. The basis: "for you have saved us from the hand of Midian" (kî hôšaʿtānū: the verb yāšaʿ again). The people's offer is not wrong in content — Gideon has indeed delivered them — but in locating the right of rule in the deliverer rather than in YHWH.
8.23: "I will not rule over you, and my son will not rule over you; the LORD will rule over you" — this is the theological center of the Gideon narrative. lō' ʾemšōl bākem…YHWH yimšōl bākem: the verb māšal (to rule, to have dominion) is the same in both clauses; the transfer is absolute. Gideon correctly identifies the theocratic principle: YHWH is Israel's king; the deliverer is his instrument, not a replacement. The tragedy of ch 8-9 is that Gideon enacts kingship in every way except the name — many wives (v30), an ephod-idol (v27), a concubine in Shechem (v31) — and his son Abimelech will take the title explicitly.
8.24: "Give me each man an earring from his plunder" — ʾezmᵊrôt zāhāb (golden earrings). The note that the Midianites were Ishmaelites (kî yiśmᵊʾēlîm hēm) explains the golden earrings — Ishmaelites were known as traders and gold-workers (cf. Gen 37:25-28). The voluntary collection suggests Gideon's personal request is an act of charismatic authority rather than legal taxation.
8.25: "We will gladly give them" — nātôn nittēn: the infinitive absolute construction intensifies the willingness — "we will absolutely give them." The garment spread out (śimla) as a collection vessel is a practical detail that authenticates the scene; it also connects to garments spread before kings (2 Kgs 9:13) — an unconscious royal gesture from the people who just offered Gideon a crown.
8.26: Seventeen hundred shekels of gold — besides the crescent ornaments, pendants, and purple garments — the itemized list: śahᵃrōnîm (crescent moon amulets), nᵊṭîpôt (pendant ornaments), bigdê ha'argāmān (purple garments of the Midianite kings), hānᵊṭîpôt (necklaces of the camels). The total wealth is enormous; the detail of "purple garments" marks the Midianite kings as royal figures. All of this becomes the raw material for apostasy.
8.27: Gideon made an ephod from it and placed it in Ophrah — an ʾēpôd in its priestly function was a garment worn by the high priest, holding the Urim and Thummim. Here it is a free-standing cultic object placed in a city — functioning as an unauthorized oracle shrine. wayyizᵊnū kol-yiśrā'ēl ʾaḥărāyw šām: all Israel committed spiritual adultery (zānāh, the standard term for covenant infidelity) after it there. wayhî lᵊGidʿōn ûlᵊbêtô lᵊmôqēš: it became a snare (môqēš) to Gideon and his house — the same word used in Exod 23:33 for idols as a snare.
8.28: The land had rest forty years in the days of Gideon — wayyishtaqeṭ hā'āreṣ ʾarbaʿîm šānāh: the land rested/was quiet. The forty-year rest is the standard sabbatical period in Judges (Othniel 40 years, Deborah 40 years, here 40 years). Forty years is a generation — one full cycle before apostasy resumes. The rest is YHWH's gift; its end with Gideon's death confirms that the rest was tied to his person, not to any institutional transformation.
8.29: Jerubbaal the son of Joash went and lived in his own house — the formulaic withdrawal to private life after the deliverer's work is done mirrors the pattern of all major judges. "Lived in his own house" (wayyēšeb bᵊbêtô) is also the phrase used for kings who have consolidated power; here it ironically anticipates the household drama of ch 9.
8.30: Gideon had seventy sons, his own offspring, for he had many wives — seventy sons (šibʿîm bānîm yōṣᵊʾê yᵊrēkô, from his own loins) plus a concubine's son. The seventy is a loaded number: seventy elders of Israel (Num 11:16), seventy descendants of Jacob (Gen 46:27), seventy nations in Gen 10. The seventy sons will be massacred down to one (Abimelech) in ch 9 — a catastrophic inversion of the founding promise to Abraham's numerous descendants.
8.31: His concubine who was in Shechem bore him Abimelech — ʾăbîmelek means "my father is king" — a name whose irony is dense: Gideon refused the title of king (v23) but named his son "my father is king." The concubine's Shechemite origin is significant: Shechem was the center of Baal-berith worship (9:4), and Abimelech will leverage his Shechemite half-identity to seize power there. The seed of apostasy is planted in Gideon's own household.
8.32: Gideon died at a good old age — ûmāt Gidʿōn ben-Yôʾāš bᵊśêbāh ṭôbāh: the formula "good old age" (śêbāh ṭôbāh) appears for Abraham (Gen 15:15), Ishmael (Gen 25:8), and David's officers — it marks a life completed with honor. Buried in the tomb of his father Joash at Ophrah of the Abiezrites: ancestral burial as the final marker of belonging to the covenant community.
8.33: "As soon as Gideon died, the people of Israel turned again" — wayhî ka'ăšer mēt Gidʿōn wayyāšūbū: the causative connection is immediate and explicit. Baalîm (the plural of Baal) and specifically Baʿal-bᵊrît (Lord of the Covenant) — a Canaanite deity whose name mocks YHWH's covenant relationship with Israel by applying the covenant vocabulary (bᵊrît) to a pagan god. This apostasy is located at Shechem, where Abimelech will receive his blood-money from Baal-berith's temple (9:4).
8.34: "Did not remember the LORD their God, who had delivered them from all their enemies" — lō' zākᵊrū (they did not remember): forgetting (šākaḥ) and not-remembering (lō' zākar) are the root sins of the Deuteronomic covenant. The participle hammôšîʿ (the one who saves/saves them) is a title for YHWH — the same root as the names Joshua/Jesus. Forgetting the Savior is the primary covenant breach.
8.35: "They did not show hesed to the family of Jerubbaal" — wᵊlō' ʿāśū ḥesed ʿim-bêt Yᵊrubbāʿal: ḥesed (covenant loyalty, steadfast love, faithful kindness) is the central covenant virtue; its failure here is both a social crime (not honoring Gideon's family) and a theological one (ḥesed toward YHWH's servant mirrors ḥesed toward YHWH). The book closes Gideon's narrative with the failure of both vertical ḥesed (v34, forgetting YHWH) and horizontal ḥesed (v35, abandoning Gideon's family) — the double collapse of covenant faithfulness.

9.1: wayyēlek ʾăḇîmeleḵ ben-yərubbaʿal šəkemāh ʾel-ʾăḥê ʾimmô — 'Abimelech son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem to his mother's brothers.' ʾăḇîmeleḵ (H40) means 'my father is king' — an ironic name for a man who will seize kingship in his father's memory, despite Gideon's explicit refusal of dynastic succession (8:23: 'I will not rule over you, nor shall my son rule over you; YHWH will rule over you'). Shechem (šekem H7927) is the ancient covenant city where Israel renewed covenant with YHWH at the end of Joshua (Josh 24) — its selection as the headquarters of Israel's first kingship attempt is deliberate anti-covenant geography.
9.2: mî-ṭôḇ lākem hāmšōl bākem šibʿîm ʾîš kōl bənê-yərubbaʿal ʾim-māšol-bākem ʾîš ʾeḥāḏ — 'Which is better for you: that seventy men rule over you — all the sons of Jerubbaal — or that one man rule over you?' māšal (H4910, to rule/have dominion) is the key political verb: Abimelech is campaigning for a kind of rule that was itself contested. His rhetoric presents a false binary: either 70 joint rulers (chaos) or one strong man (stability). The emotional appeal — 'he is your bone and flesh' (ʿaṣmêkem ûḇəśarkêkem) — co-opts the covenant kinship formula (Gen 2:23; 2 Sam 5:1) for political manipulation.
9.4: wayyittənû-lô šibʿîm kāsep mibêt baʿal bərît wayyiškor bāhem ʾăḇîmeleḵ ʾănāšîm rêqîm ûpōḥăzîm — 'They gave him seventy pieces of silver from the temple of Baal-Berith, and Abimelech hired worthless and reckless men with them.' bêt baʿal bərît (H1168 H1285 — House of Baal-of-the-Covenant/Baal-Berith) is the pagan covenant-shrine at Shechem — the contrast with YHWH's covenant at the same city (Josh 24) is the text's sharpest irony. rêqîm (H7386, empty/worthless men) and pōḥăzîm (H6348 root, reckless/frivolous) describe hired mercenaries with no covenant loyalties — the army you get when you fund kingship with Baal's money.
9.5: wayyāḇōʾ ḇêt ʾāḇîw ʿoprātāh wayyahărog ʾet-ʾeḥāyw bənê-yərubbaʿal šibʿîm ʾîš ʿal-ʾeḇen ʾaḥāt — 'He went to his father's house at Ophrah and killed his brothers the sons of Jerubbaal — seventy men — on one stone.' ʾeḇen ʾaḥāt (one stone) — execution on a single stone suggests formal-ritual killing, possibly a sacrificial stone. The killing of seventy brothers is an extreme act of dynastic consolidation paralleled in ancient Near Eastern royal succession practices (the Assyrian annals record similar fratricide). The number seventy — the full measure of Gideon's legitimate sons — is also the number of Jacob's household (Gen 46:27) and of the elders of Israel (Exod 24:1), making the massacre of all seventy a symbolic destruction of covenant Israel's leadership.
9.7: wayyaggîḏû ləyôṯām wayyēlek wayyaʿămod bərōš har-gərizîm — 'When they told Jotham, he went and stood on top of Mount Gerizim and cried aloud.' Mount Gerizim (har gərizîm H1630) is the mountain of blessing in Deuteronomy's covenant ceremony (Deut 11:29; 27:12) — the blessing mountain becomes the platform for Jotham's curse-parable. wayyiṣʿaq (H6817, he cried out loudly) — Jotham's parable is shouted down from the mountain, a prophetic proclamation rather than a private conversation. He is the sole surviving legitimate heir, speaking his father's theology.
9.8: hālôk hālkû hāʿēṣîm limšōaḥ ʿălêhem meleḵ — 'The trees went out to anoint a king over themselves.' The fable (māšal H4912 — parable/proverb/allegory) uses the device of talking trees to satirize the Shechemites' kingship project. māšaḥ (H4886, to anoint) — anointing with oil was the investiture ceremony for kings, priests, and prophets; the trees' offer to the olive, fig, and vine parodies the legitimate anointing of YHWH's chosen king. Each tree's refusal to stop producing its useful fruit to 'sway over the trees' encodes the wisdom principle: truly productive persons do not abandon their calling for empty political power.
9.9: wayyōʾmer lāhem hāzayit heḥādaltî ʾet-dišnî ʾăšer-bî yəkaḇḏû ʾĕlōhîm wăʾănāšîm ûḇāʾtî lānûaʿ ʿal-hāʿēṣîm — 'The olive tree said to them: Shall I leave my fatness (dišnî), by which gods and men are honored, and go to sway over the trees?' dešen (H1880, fatness/richness/oil) — olive oil was used in sacrifice (honoring God) and in cooking (honoring men). The olive's refusal encodes: genuine service to God and man is incompatible with the pursuit of political dominance. The fable's logic — olive, fig, vine all refuse; the bramble accepts — implies that only the useless/dangerous plant seeks to reign.
9.14: wayyōʾmərû kol-hāʿēṣîm ʾel-hāʾātāḏ — 'Then all the trees said to the bramble.' ʾātāḏ (H328, bramble/buckthorn — Rhamnus palaeostinus or Ziziphus lotus) is a thorny shrub of no fruit or timber value, useful only for starting fires. Its acceptance of the kingship offer — 'Come and take shelter in my shade' — is the parable's punchline: the bramble promises shade it cannot provide (low-growing thorny shrubs give no real shade), and threatens to destroy even the cedars of Lebanon if not obeyed. ʾēš tēṣēʾ min-hāʾātāḏ — 'fire came out from the bramble' — dry thornbush is the most flammable vegetation, legendary as a fire-starter. The warning becomes prophecy: Abimelech the bramble will destroy those who shelter under him.
9.15: wayyōʾmer hāʾātāḏ ʾel-hāʿēṣîm ʾim beʾĕmet ʾattem mōšəḥîm ʾōtî ləmeleḵ ʿălêkem — 'The bramble said to the trees: If in truth you are anointing me king over you...' beʾĕmet (H571, in truth/in faithfulness) — the bramble's challenge is a double irony: the trees cannot 'in truth' be doing this, because no genuine political community would turn to the worthless bramble. The condition is hypothetical-absurdist. bōʾû ḥăsû bəṣillî — 'come and take refuge in my shade' — ḥāsāh (H2620, to take refuge/shelter) is consistently used of taking refuge in YHWH (Ps 2:12; 5:11; 7:1). Jotham's parable implies: seeking safety in a bramble-king rather than in YHWH is the fundamental folly of Shechem's choice.
9.23: wayyišlaḥ ʾĕlōhîm rûaḥ rāʿāh bên ʾăḇîmeleḵ ûḇên baʿălê šəkem — 'God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the leaders of Shechem.' rûaḥ rāʿāh (H7307 H7451, evil/bad spirit) — the sending of a disruptive spirit as divine judicial instrument appears also in 1 Sam 16:14 (evil spirit from YHWH troubles Saul) and 1 Kgs 22:22 (a lying spirit dispatched to deceive Ahab). The theological claim is that YHWH uses fractured relationships and betrayal as instruments of judgment. bāgad (H898, to act treacherously/deal deceitfully) — Shechem's treachery against Abimelech is the mechanism of YHWH's vengeance for the murder of the seventy.
9.24: lāḇôʾ ḥămas šibʿîm bənê-yərubbaʿal wədāmām lāśûm ʿal-ʾăḇîmeleḵ ʾăḥîhem ʾăšer hārag ʾôtām — 'in order that the violence (ḥāmās) done to the seventy sons of Jerubbaal might come, and their blood be laid on Abimelech their brother.' ḥāmās (H2555, violence/wrong/injustice) is the gravest term for social/physical wrong in Hebrew — it appears in Gen 6:11 as the reason for the Flood. Blood guilt (dāmîm H1818) — the unpunished blood of the murdered brothers is a moral charge that must be resolved. The text tracks this resolution explicitly through the fable's fulfillment: fire from the bramble consumed the men of Shechem (v49), and the bramble was destroyed (v53-54).
9.53: wattiššəlēk ʾiššāh ʾaḥat pelah-rekaḇ ʿal-rōʾš ʾăḇîmeleḵ wattāroṣ ʾet-gulgultô — 'A certain woman threw an upper millstone on Abimelech's head and cracked his skull.' pelah-rekaḵ (H6400 H7393, upper millstone) — the upper millstone (the grinding stone that rides on the fixed lower stone) was a household object associated entirely with women's grain-grinding work. Its use as a weapon is as unexpected as Jael's tent peg. gulgōlet (H1538, skull) — the skull-cracking detail matches the Jael event (4:21) and forms a doublet: in Judges, great military leaders are killed by women using domestic implements, because YHWH consistently reverses human expectations of who the decisive agent will be.
9.54: wayyimhēr wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-hannaʿar nōśēʾ kēlāyw wayyōʾmer lô šlop ḥarbekā ûḏāqərēnî-ḇāh pen-yōʾmərû lî ʾiššāh hărāgātəhû — 'He quickly called to the young man who carried his armor and said: Draw your sword and kill me, so that they will not say of me: a woman killed him.' Abimelech's final concern is his reputation — he does not want the historical record to say a woman killed him. The irony is absolute: by making this request explicit he ensures the story records exactly what he feared. Hebrews 11 includes Barak but not Abimelech; the anti-king's death at a woman's hand is the text's final verdict on a life built on false power.
9.57: wəʾēt kol-rāʿat ʾanšê šekem hēšîḇ ʾĕlōhîm bərōʾšām wayyāḇōʾ ʾălêhem qillalat yôṯām ben-yərubbaʿal — 'God also made all the evil of the men of Shechem return on their heads, and on them came the curse of Jotham son of Jerubbaal.' šûḇ (H7725, to return/bring back) — divine justice operates as return: what was done comes back. qəlālāh (H7045, curse — from qālal H7043, to be light/curse) is the formal covenant curse that Jotham pronounced from Gerizim (the blessing-mountain turned into a curse-platform). The narrative closes with explicit divine attribution — not fate or history but YHWH-through-Elohim who executes the curse's fulfillment.

10.1: wayyāqom ʾaḥărê ʾăḇîmeleḵ ləhôšîaʿ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl tôlāʿ ben-pûʾāh ben-dôḏô ʾîš yiśśāśkār — 'After Abimelech, Tola the son of Puah, son of Dodo, a man of Issachar, rose to save Israel.' ləhôšîaʿ (H3467, to save/deliver) — the judge's essential function stated succinctly; qûm (H6965, to arise/rise up) is the standard verb for YHWH raising up a deliverer. Tola's genealogy (son of Puah, son of Dodo) goes three generations — unusual depth for a minor judge, suggesting scribal memory of an actual historical figure. His name means 'worm' or 'crimson/scarlet' (tôlāʿ H8438 — the same word for the crimson dye from the cochineal insect, used in the tabernacle textiles, Exod 25:4).
10.3: wayyāqom ʾaḥărāyw yāʾîr haggileʿādî wayyišpōṭ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl ʿeśrîm ûšəṭayim šānāh — 'After him arose Jair the Gileadite, who judged Israel twenty-two years.' šāpaṭ (H8199, to judge) — the minor judges are described by this verb without accompanying military narrative. gilʿādî (Gileadite — from Gilead, H1568, the Transjordan tribal territory) — Jair's origin in the Transjordan is consistent with the geographic breadth of Israel's settlement. His thirty sons riding on thirty donkeys (v4) — ʿayārîm H5895, young donkeys — is a status marker: donkey-riding was aristocratic in pre-horse Israel (cf. Judg 5:10 — those who ride on white donkeys). The thirty cities named 'Havvoth-jair' (H2334) are his legacy, preserving his name in the landscape.
10.6: wayyōsîpû bənê yiśrāʾēl laʿăśôt hāraʿ bəʿênê YHWH wayyaʿabdû ʾet-habbəʿālîm wəʾet-hāʿaštārôt wəʾet ʾĕlōhê ʾărām wəʾet ʾĕlōhê ṣîḏôn wəʾet ʾĕlōhê môʾāḇ wəʾet ʾĕlōhê bənê ʿammôn wəʾet ʾĕlōhê pəlištîm — the longest and most explicit list of foreign deities in Judges. Seven specific national pantheons are named: bəʿālîm (Baals — plural form of Baal H1168, the storm/fertility deity), ʿaštārôt (Ashtaroth — H6252, plural of Ashtoreth/Astarte, the goddess of love and war), ʾĕlōhê ʾărām (gods of Aram — Syrian pantheon including Hadad), ʾĕlōhê ṣîḏôn (gods of Sidon — Phoenician deities including Baal and Asherah), ʾĕlōhê môʾāḇ (gods of Moab — primarily Chemosh H3645), ʾĕlōhê ʿammôn (gods of Ammon — primarily Molech/Milcom H4445), ʾĕlōhê pəlištîm (gods of the Philistines — primarily Dagon H1712). The exhaustive list emphasizes the comprehensiveness of Israel's apostasy: every surrounding people's deity has been embraced.
10.7: wayyiḥar-ʾap YHWH bəyiśrāʾēl wayyimkərēm bəyaḏ-pəlištîm ûbəyaḏ bənê ʿammôn — 'The anger of YHWH burned against Israel, and he sold them into the hands of the Philistines and into the hands of the sons of Ammon.' ḥārāh ʾap (H2734 H639, the anger/nose burned — idiom for divine wrath) — the burning-nose idiom for anger is physiological: flared nostrils, flushed face. The dual oppression — Philistines (west) AND Ammon (east/Transjordan) — means Israel is attacked from two directions simultaneously, the most severe judgment yet. mākar (H4376, to sell) — again the commercial metaphor for YHWH's judicial action: Israel's apostasy is a breach of covenant that results in covenant curses (Deut 28:36 — 'YHWH will bring you and your king to a nation that neither you nor your fathers have known').
10.10: wayyiṣʿaqû bənê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-YHWH lēʾmōr ḥāṭāʾnû lāk kî-ʿāzaḇnû ʾet-ʾĕlōhênû wanaʿăḇōd ʾet-habbəʿālîm — 'The sons of Israel cried out to YHWH, saying: We have sinned against you, for we have forsaken our God and served the Baals.' This is the most explicit corporate confession in Judges: ḥāṭāʾnû lāk (we have sinned to/against you — the covenant-violation formula), ʿāzaḇ (H5800, to forsake/abandon — the covenant desertion term opposite of dāḇaq to cling/cleave to, Deut 10:20), ʿāḇad (H5647, to serve — the word for both slave-labor and worship; to worship Baal is to be enslaved to Baal). The confession identifies the sin precisely: not merely wrongdoing but covenant infidelity — forsaking a person (YHWH) for a rival.
10.14: ləkû wəziʿaqû ʾel-hāʾĕlōhîm ʾăšer bəḥartem bāhem hēmmāh yôšîʿû lākem bəʿēt ṣāratkêm — 'Go and cry out to the gods whom you have chosen. Let them save you in your time of distress.' YHWH's sarcasm — 'go cry to the gods you chose' — is covenant-lawsuit rhetoric (rîḇ H7379 pattern). The verb bāḥar (H977, to choose) echoes Deuteronomy's repeated assertion that Israel chose YHWH (Deut 26:17-18) and YHWH chose Israel — here the choice is inverted: Israel chose foreign gods and must now experience the logical consequence of that choice. ṣārāh (H6869, distress/trouble/straits) — the gods of Ammon/Philistia cannot deliver from ṣārāh; only YHWH, the God of the Exodus, can.
10.16: wayyāsîrû ʾet-ʾĕlōhê hannēkār miqqirḇām wayyaʿabdû ʾet-YHWH wattiqqṣar napšô bəʿămal yiśrāʾēl — 'They put away the foreign gods from among them and served YHWH, and he became impatient over the misery of Israel.' ʾĕlōhê hannēkār (H5236, foreign gods — gods of the strange/alien — these are the gods of the other nations, not just any unknown deity; the term nēkār implies both foreignness and strangeness). wattiqqṣar napšô — 'his soul/being was shortened/impatient' — qāṣar (H7114, to be short/cut short; with nepeš it means to feel distress, to be grieved, to be unable to endure — cf. Num 21:4 where Israel's soul 'became short' on the way). YHWH is not a detached judge but a personal being capable of emotional response to human suffering. This verse is one of the most striking expressions of divine pathos in the OT: Israel's ʿāmal (H5999, toil/misery/trouble — the word for the crushing labor of oppression) registers in YHWH's own inner life.

1.1: wayyišʾălû bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl bĕYHWH — 'the sons of Israel inquired of YHWH.' The verb šāʾal (ask, inquire) denotes oracular consultation — seeking YHWH's directive before military action. It is the same root as the name Šāʾûl (Saul). The failure to inquire at Gibeon (Josh 9:14) brought disaster; here its presence opens the book rightly. The question 'Who shall go up first?' reflects the need to know the vanguard role in a post-Joshua transition.
1.2: YHWH ʾāmar yĕhûdāh yaʿăleh hinnēh nātattî ʾet-hāʾāreṣ bĕyādô — 'YHWH said: Judah shall go up; behold, I have given the land into his hand.' The nātattî is the divine proleptic perfect — a qatal form expressing certainty of future fulfillment as though already accomplished. The verb precedes any human action; YHWH's giving renders the outcome certain before the first battle.
1.3: Judah invites Simeon: ûbāʾtā ʾittî bĕgôrālî — 'come up with me into my allotted portion.' The gôrāl (lot, allotted territory) is the distributive term from the Joshua allotments. Simeon's territory was embedded within Judah's (Josh 19:1), making the partnership geographic as well as covenantal; their cooperation reflects mutual obligation within the distribution Joshua authorized.
1.4: wayyittēn YHWH ʾet-hakkĕnaʿănî wĕʾet-happĕrizzî bĕyādām — 'YHWH gave the Canaanites and the Perizzites into their hand.' The wayyiqtol narrative places YHWH as subject of the decisive giving (nātan bĕyād = give into the hand), the standard conquest-gift formula. Israel strikes, but YHWH gives — the syntactic subjects carry the theological weight.
1.5: ʾĂdōnî-bezeq — 'lord of Bezek.' The title ʾădōnî (my lord) is a throne-title rather than a personal name, marking the political claim being contested. The Canaanite/Perizzite leadership at Bezek is signaled by its lordship-title format, common in ANE city-state governance.
1.6: Adoni-bezek fled (wayyānos) but was caught and had thumbs and big toes cut off (bĕhōnôt yādāyw wĕraglāyw). The verb qissēṣ (pi'el of qāṣaṣ, cut off, sever) describes a standard ANE humiliation of captured enemy commanders: removing thumbs prevented sword-grip; removing big toes prevented stable combat stance. The procedure was degradation rather than execution.
1.7: šibʿîm mĕlākîm bĕhōnôt yĕdêhem wĕraglêhem mĕqussāṣîm — 'seventy kings with their thumbs and big toes cut off.' Adoni-bezek's self-condemnation applies the lex talionis principle: kăʾašer ʿāśîtî kēn šillem lî ʾĕlōhîm — 'as I have done, so God has repaid me.' The verb šillem (pi'el of šālam, make complete, pay in full, requite) is the same root as šālôm; justice restores the wholeness violated by the original act. Seventy is formulaic for completeness rather than a precise count.
1.8: wayyillaḥămû bĕnê yĕhûdāh bîrûšālaim wayyilkĕdû ʾōtāh — 'the men of Judah fought against Jerusalem and captured it.' The verb lākad (capture, seize) is the standard conquest term. The apparent contradiction with v.21 (Benjamin did not expel the Jebusites) likely reflects temporary capture and burning followed by Jebusite reoccupation — a pattern persisting until David (2 Sam 5:6-9).
1.9: After Jerusalem, Judah campaigns across three geographic zones: hāhār wĕhannegeb wĕhašĕpēlāh — the hill country, the Negeb, and the Shephelah. These three terms cover the full vertical and horizontal range of Judah's allotment, signaling systematic territorial consolidation organized by terrain type rather than political boundaries.
1.10: wĕšēm-ḥebrôn lĕpānîm qiryat-ʾarbāʿ — the former name of Hebron was Kiriath-arba. Qiryat-ʾarbāʿ means either 'city of Arba' (the great Anakim ancestor, Josh 14:15) or 'city of four.' The use of former names is a recurring narrative device in the conquest accounts, marking the historical transformation of cities and territories by their renaming.
1.11: From Hebron, Judah attacks Debir (dĕbîr), formerly qiryat-sēper — 'city of the scroll/book.' The name sēper (scroll, document) suggests a Bronze Age scribal or administrative center, consistent with ANE evidence for scribal cities. The transition from book-city to judge's conquest marks the reorientation of literacy and administration under the covenant people.
1.12: Caleb's offer: ʾašer-yakkeh ʾet-qiryat-sēper ûlĕkādāh wĕnātattî lô ʾet-ʿaksāh bittî lĕʾiššāh — 'whoever strikes and captures Kiriath-sepher, I will give him Achsah my daughter as wife.' The marriage-prize formula for military valor recurs in 1 Sam 18:17 (Saul's offer to David). The narrative's interest is not Caleb's cultural convention but Achsah's subsequent agency in v.15.
1.13: Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother (ʾăḥî kālēb haqqāṭōn), captures the city. Qāṭōn (younger/smaller) echoes the pattern of the younger brother succeeding over older expectations (Jacob, Joseph, David). Othniel's Kenizzite descent makes him a non-Israelite ethnic incorporated into the covenant community — the same outsider-integration as Caleb himself (Josh 14:6).
1.14: wattĕsîtēhû liš'ôl mēʾēt-ʾābîhā śādeh — 'she urged him to ask her father for a field.' The verb sûṯ (hiph, urge, incite) is used elsewhere for inciting to idolatry (Deut 13:7), but here positively for Achsah's initiative. wattispōaḥ mēʿal haḥămôr — 'she dismounted from her donkey' — the dismounting gesture formally signals a petition is coming, setting up the verbal request.
1.15: Achsah's request: hābāh-lî bĕrākāh kî ʾereṣ hannegeb nĕtattnî wĕnātattāh lî gullōt mayim — 'give me a blessing; since you have put me in the dry land, give me springs of water.' The word bĕrākāh means both blessing and gift/present — her request is itself a blessing-claim. The gullōt mayim (springs, literally bowls/basins of water) she requests are the ʿelyônôt and taḥtiyyônôt (upper and lower springs). Caleb gives both; the narrative rewards precise and bold petitioning.
1.16: The Kenites, descendants of Moses's father-in-law (ḥōtēn mōšeh), settle with Judah in the Negev of Arad. The ʾîr hattĕmārîm (city of palms) is the Jericho area. Ḥōtēn (father-in-law) marks the Mosaic family connection; the Kenite alliance (cf. Num 10:29-32) represents the ongoing incorporation of non-Israelite groups into the covenant community through loyalty relationships.
1.17: wayyaḥărîmû ʾōtāh — they devoted Zephath to ḥērem (destruction). The hiph'il of ḥāram marks sacred exclusion — the devoted city becomes YHWH's exclusively. wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šēm-hāʿîr ḥormāh — it was renamed Hormah from the same root (ḥerem), making the name a permanent lexical memorial of the devotion to destruction: the city's identity becomes its fate.
1.18: Judah captures Gaza, Ashkelon, and Ekron — three of the five Philistine city-states. The LXX reads 'did not capture' rather than 'captured,' and the conflict with Josh 13:3 and Judg 3:3 (Philistine lords still active) supports the LXX reading. The coastal plain remained effectively Philistine territory throughout the judges period despite momentary successes.
1.19: wayhî YHWH ʾet-yĕhûdāh wayyōreš ʾet-hāhār kî lōʾ lĕhôrîš ʾet-yōšĕbê hāʿēmeq kî-rekeb barzel lāhem — 'YHWH was with Judah; he took the hill country, but could not drive out the valley inhabitants for they had iron chariots.' The lōʾ lĕhôrîš failure formula appears here first; it will recur through v.35. The rekeb barzel (iron chariots) represent Late Bronze/Iron I military technology giving tactical superiority in open terrain — the theological tension (YHWH with them, yet failure) is the honest reckoning of incomplete obedience meeting incomplete faith.
1.20: Caleb drives out the three sons of Anak from Hebron (wayyōreš miššām ʾet-šĕlōšâ bĕnê hāʿănāq). The Anakim had terrorized the wilderness spies (Num 13:33); Caleb's victory completes what Moses had specifically promised him (Josh 14:12). The phrase kăʾašer dibber mōšeh (as Moses had said) marks this as a fulfilled-promise event, contrasting with the partial failures surrounding it.
1.21: wĕʾet-hayyĕbûsî yôšēb yĕrûšālaim lōʾ hôrîšû bĕnê binyāmîn — 'Benjamin did not drive out the Jebusites from Jerusalem.' This is the first tribal failure report. The Jebusite persistence explains why Jerusalem remained a neutral city between north and south until David's capture (2 Sam 5:6-9). The lōʾ hôrîš (did not drive out) formula frames the failure as disobedience rather than military incapacity.
1.22: wayyaʿălû bêt-yôsēp gam-hēm bêt-ʾēl waYHWH ʿimmām — 'the house of Joseph went up, and YHWH was with them.' The divine-presence formula YHWH ʿimmām is the decisive variable: its presence here signals success before any battle description. Its implicit absence from the tribal failure reports explains those failures. Throughout the DtrH, YHWH ʿimmô/ʿimmām is the single most important predictor of outcome.
1.23: The house of Joseph sends scouts to Bethel, formerly called Lûz. The name change from Luz to Bethel (house of God) was made by Jacob after his vision there (Gen 28:19). The narrative's use of both names (former and current) preserves the theological memory — the place of divine encounter is now a military objective.
1.24: The scouts offer the man ḥesed in exchange for information: wĕʿāśînû ʿimmĕkā ḥāsed — 'we will deal with you in covenant loyalty.' The word ḥesed (covenant fidelity, loyal love) here functions as a pledge of protection — the same covenant-loyalty exchange as with Rahab and the Jericho spies (Josh 2:12-14). The Rahab parallel is deliberate: another city's opening provided by an insider who receives ḥesed.
1.25: The man showed them the entrance; they struck the city (wayyakkû ʾet-hāʿîr lĕpî-ḥāreb) but released (šillaḥ) the man and his family. The verb šillaḥ (send away, release, set free) — used for freeing prisoners and slaves — fulfills the ḥesed pledge completely. The man is not merely spared but released with freedom, exactly as promised.
1.26: The man went to the land of the Hittites and built a city he named Lûz — preserving the old name in new territory. The founding of a city in Hittite territory named Luz marks the geographic exile of Canaanite culture: driven from Canaan but persistent in the north. The verse records cultural survival-in-exile as historical observation, not condemnation.
1.27: The Manasseh failure list: wĕlōʾ-hôrîš mĕnaššeh ʾet-bêt-šĕʾān — 'Manasseh did not drive out Beth-shean.' The formula lōʾ hôrîš + city list structures the remainder of ch.1. Beth-shean controlled the Jordan Valley pass; Taanach and Megiddo controlled the Jezreel Valley. These strategic chokepoints remaining in Canaanite hands fragmented Israel's territory into disconnected highlands.
1.28: wayhî kî-ḥāzaq yiśrāʾēl wayyāśem ʾet-hakkĕnaʿănî lāmas wĕhôrêš lōʾ hôrîšô — 'when Israel grew strong, they put the Canaanites to forced labor, but did not drive them out.' The verb māras (forced labor, corvée) reflects economic exploitation of those not expelled. Strength was used for commercial benefit rather than covenant obedience — tolerating the prohibited because it was profitable.
1.29: wĕʾeprayim lōʾ hôrîš ʾet-hakkĕnaʿănî hayyôšēb bĕgāzer — Ephraim did not drive out the Canaanites at Gezer. Gezer remained a contested city until the Solomonic period, when Pharaoh took it and gave it as dowry (1 Kgs 9:16). The city's position at the coastal plain's edge meant Ephraim's territory had a permanent enclave it could not control.
1.30: Zebulun did not drive out the inhabitants of Kitron or Nahalol — wayyēšeb hakkĕnaʿănî bĕqirbô wayhî lāmas — 'the Canaanites lived in his midst and became forced laborers.' The shift from expulsion-language to coexistence-language marks the accommodation: pragmatic exploitation instead of covenant obedience. The repeated formula documents systematic tribal compromise at the policy level.
1.31: Asher's failure list is the longest — six cities including Acco (Mediterranean port), Sidon (major Phoenician city-state), and four others. The coastal territory of Asher was economically rich but militarily dominated by Phoenician cities. The length of the list signals Asher's deepest accommodation with coastal Canaanite culture of any tribe.
1.32: wayyēšeb hāʾašērî bĕqereb hakkĕnaʿănî — 'the Asherites lived among the Canaanites.' The preposition shifts from earlier: other tribes had Canaanites living in their midst; for Asher, Israel lives among the Canaanites. The balance of cultural power has inverted — Asher is the minority living within the Canaanite world, not the majority tolerating pockets of non-expulsion.
1.33: Naphtali lived among the Canaanites at Beth-shemesh (bêt-šemeš = house of the sun, a solar cult site) and Beth-anath (bêt-ʿănāt = house of Anat, the Canaanite war goddess). The names of the cities Naphtali failed to expel expose the religious stakes: coexistence with solar and war-goddess cult centers is the seedbed of the syncretism Judges will diagnose throughout the period.
1.34: wayyilḥăṣû hāʾĕmōrî ʾet-bĕnê dān hāhārāh — 'the Amorites pressed the people of Dan back into the hill country.' Dan is unique: the only tribe actively driven back rather than merely failing to drive out. The Amorites' offensive confinement of Dan to the hills explains the Danite migration to the far north in ch.18 — their original allotment became militarily untenable.
1.35: The Amorites persisted in Mount Heres and nearby cities until wattikbad yad bêt-yôsēp — 'the hand of the house of Joseph prevailed.' The idiom kābad yād (heavy/strong hand) signals dominant power — the same root as kābōd (glory/weight). Even Joseph's success produces subjugation (lāmas) rather than expulsion — the strongest tribe still settles for economic exploitation instead of the covenant command.
1.36: ûgĕbûl hāʾĕmōrî mimmāʿăleh ʿaqrabbîm minhassela wāmāʿlāh — 'the border of the Amorites ran from the ascent of Akrabbim.' The Scorpion Pass (maʿăleh ʿaqrabbîm) marks the southern boundary of persistent Amorite territory (cf. Num 34:4; Josh 15:3). The closing geographic note confirms that the conquest ended with defined Amorite enclaves intact — the map was not fully redrawn.

3.1: wĕʾēlleh haggôyim ʾašer hinnîaḥ YHWH lĕnassôt bām ʾet-yiśrāʾēl — 'these are the nations YHWH left to test Israel.' The verb hinnîaḥ (hiph of nûaḥ, to leave, allow to remain) signals intentional preservation — these nations are not accidental leftovers but deliberately maintained instruments of divine testing and discipline.
3.2: raq lĕmaʿan daʿat dōrôt bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl lĕlammĕdām milḥāmāh — 'only so that generations of Israel might know war and be taught it.' Two purposes: covenant testing (v.4) and military education for generations with no Exodus memory. The pedagogical framing acknowledges divine sovereignty over historical hardship — no Israelite suffering is unintentional in this period.
3.3: The enemy roster: the five Philistine lords (sĕrānê pĕlištîm), Canaanites, Sidonians, and Hivites. Sārēn is the specific title of the Philistine pentapolis rulers — likely an Indo-European loanword reflecting the Philistines' Aegean origins. The geographic range from the Philistine coast to the Lebanon mountains maps the entire unconquered perimeter surrounding Israel's highland core.
3.4: wayhî lĕnassôt bām ʾet-yiśrāʾēl lādaʿat hăyišmĕʿû ʾet-miṣwôt YHWH — 'to test Israel, to find out whether they would obey the commandments.' The infinitive lādaʿat (to know, to find out) marks experiential verification — the test produces knowledge that theory cannot. The object of the test: whether šāmaʿ (obey, hear-and-heed) would operate under the specific pressure of living among unconquered peoples.
3.5: ûbĕnê yiśrāʾēl yāšĕbû bĕqereb hakkĕnaʿănî — 'the people of Israel lived in the midst of the Canaanites.' The preposition bĕqereb (in the midst, within) signals the conquest's reversal: instead of Canaanites being expelled from Israel's midst, Israel inhabits the Canaanite world. The seven-nation list catalogs the full breadth of the cultural-religious environment Israel chose to inhabit.
3.6: wayyiqqĕḥû ʾet-bĕnôtêhem lāhem lĕnāšîm... wayyaʿabdû ʾet-ʾĕlōhêhem — 'they took their daughters... gave their daughters... and served their gods.' The intermarriage-idolatry chain is the exact sequence Deuteronomy 7:3-4 warned against: 'do not intermarry... for they will turn your sons away to serve other gods.' The verb chain (took, gave, served) follows Deuteronomy's predicted causality exactly.
3.7: wayyiškĕḥû ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhêhem — 'they forgot the LORD their God.' The verb šākaḥ (forget) in Deuteronomy describes active neglect, not innocent forgetting: Deut 8:11-19 identifies forgetting YHWH as the covenant danger of prosperity; Deut 8:19 makes it the condition for perishing. Hāʾăšērôt are the sacred wooden poles associated with Asherah, Canaanite consort of El — the female divine pillar planted in the ground as a worship object.
3.8: wayyiḥar-ʾap YHWH bĕyiśrāʾēl wayyimkĕrēm bĕyad kûšan-rišʿātayim — 'YHWH sold them into the hand of Cushan-rishathaim.' The verb mākar (sell) is stark: Israel becomes chattel, transferred in a transaction. The king's name kûšan rišʿātayim ('Cushan of double wickedness') is almost certainly a Hebrew satirical gloss on his actual name — OT authors regularly distort enemies' names. Rišʿātayim (dual of rišʿāh) = doubly wicked, making the title a theological verdict on the oppressor.
3.9: wayyizʿăqû bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl ʾel-YHWH wayyāqem YHWH môšîaʿ lĕbĕnê-yiśrāʾēl — 'the Israelites cried out to YHWH, and YHWH raised up a deliverer.' The verb zāʿaq (cry out, cry for help) is the vocabulary of oppressed people in extremity (Exod 3:7; Ps 34:17). The covenantal hinge: cry → raised deliverer. Môšîaʿ (participle of yāšaʿ, one who saves) is the Othniel narrative's central term and the paradigm that every subsequent judge repeats with variations.
3.10: wattĕhî ʿālāyw rûaḥ YHWH wayyišpōṭ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl wayyēṣēʾ lammilḥāmāh — 'the Spirit of YHWH came upon him, he judged Israel, and he went out to war.' The rûaḥ YHWH (Spirit/wind of YHWH) is the empowering presence that equips the judge. The Spirit precedes and motivates the action; the sequence — Spirit comes → judgment → warfare — establishes the theological pattern for all subsequent judge narratives. The judges' effectiveness is divine endowment, not personal charisma.
3.11: wayyišqōṭ hāʾāreṣ ʾarbaʿîm šānāh wayyāmot ʿotnîʾēl ben-qĕnaz — 'the land had rest forty years; then Othniel died.' Šāqaṭ (have rest, be quiet) is the Judges peace-formula after each deliverer's work. The forty-year period is the formulaic generation. Othniel's narrative is the most schematic — no personal flaws, no complications — establishing the ideal-type against which all subsequent judges are implicitly measured.
3.12: wayyōsipû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl laʿăśôt hāraʿ bĕʿênê YHWH — 'they again did evil.' Yāsap (again, additionally) signals iteration: the cycle restarts. wayyĕḥazzēq YHWH ʾet-ʿeglôn... ʿal-yiśrāʾēl — 'YHWH strengthened Eglon against Israel.' The verb ḥizzēq (strengthen) — YHWH actively empowers the oppressor as instrument of discipline. The enemy's strength is not Israel's military failure but YHWH's sovereign appointment.
3.13: Eglon gathered Ammonites and Amalekites, defeating Israel and taking ʾîr hattĕmārîm (city of palms = Jericho). The symbolic weight: the first city of the conquest — taken by miracle — recaptured by Moabite coalition. The Jericho reversal measures the depth of Israel's regression from the Joshua ideal.
3.14: wayyaʿabdû bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl ʾet-ʿeglôn melek-môʾāb šĕmōneh ʿeśrēh šānāh — eighteen years of ʿābad (serve/worship) directed to the Moabite king. The covenant-service word that should go only to YHWH is directed to the enemy; Israel's refusal to serve YHWH produces servitude to the oppressor. The choice of whom to serve is never neutral.
3.15: wayyāqem YHWH lāhem môšîaʿ ʾet-ʾēhûd ben-gērāʾ ben-hayĕmînî ʾîš ʾiṭṭēr yad-yĕmînô — Ehud described as 'bound/restricted in his right hand' (ʾiṭṭēr yad-yĕmînô) = left-handed. The irony: a Benjaminite (ben-yāmîn = son of the right hand) who uses his left hand. His 'disability' — overlooked by the guards checking the standard sword-side — is the key to the assassination. YHWH consistently works through the unexpected and overlooked.
3.16: wayyaʿaś lô ʾēhûd ḥereb ûlāh šĕnê pîyôt gōmed ʾārkāh — a double-edged sword a gōmed (hapax legomenon, estimated 30-40 cm) in length. Šĕnê pîyôt (two edges, literally two mouths) — peh (mouth) used for a blade's edge is a common Semitic metaphor (cf. Heb 4:12 for a sword with two edges). The weapon's design — short, double-edged, concealable on the unexpected thigh — reflects deliberate planning.
3.17: wayyaqdēb ʾet-hamminḥāh lĕʿeglôn melek-môʾāb wĕʿeglôn ʾîš bārîʾ mĕʾōd — 'he presented the tribute to Eglon... Eglon was a very fat man.' The name ʿeglôn (round one, calf) from ʿēgel (calf) — a narrator's irony planted in the first description. Bārîʾ (fat, stout) is clinically observed; the same word for Pharaoh's fat cows (Gen 41:2). The detail functions as a plot mechanism: Eglon's obesity allows the blade to disappear into the wound (v.22).
3.18: After presenting the tribute Ehud sent away the bearers (wayyĕšallaḥ ʾet-hāʿām nōśĕʾê hamminḥāh). The deliberate pacing creates narrative suspense: preparation (sword made, v.16) → presentation (tribute offered, v.17) → entourage dismissed (v.18) → return alone (v.19). The narrator releases information at exactly the rate of the protagonist's plan.
3.19: wĕhûʾ šāb min-happĕsilîm ʾašer ʾet-haggilgāl — Ehud turned back from the pĕsilîm (carved idols/standing stones) at Gilgal. The idolatrous standing stones at the conquest's covenant-renewal site miniaturize the book's syncretism theme. dĕbar-sēter lî ʾēlêkā — 'I have a secret word for you' — sēter (hidden, secret) echoes prophetic reception language; Ehud uses dĕbar ʾĕlōhîm (word of God) in v.20 to gain private audience.
3.20: wĕhûʾ yôšēb bǎʿăliyyat hammĕqērāh ʾašer lô lĕbaddô — Eglon alone in his cool roof chamber. The privative lĕbaddô (alone, by himself) is the assassin's gift. dĕbar ʾĕlōhîm lî ʾēlêkā — 'I have a word of God for you.' Ehud appropriates the prophetic speech-formula (dĕbar ʾĕlōhîm) for lethal purpose; Eglon's respectful rising to receive the divine word places him in the posture the sword requires.
3.21: wayyišlaḥ ʾēhûd ʾet-yad haśśĕmōʾl wayyiqqaḥ ʾet-haḥereb mēʿal yĕrek yĕmînô wayyitqāʿehā bĕbiṭnô — 'Ehud reached with his left hand, took the sword from his right thigh, and thrust it into his belly.' The left hand is highlighted throughout as the operative agent. Guards searched the left thigh (normal sword-side); the right thigh carried no weapon expectation. The verb tāqaʿ (thrust, drive sharply) is onomatopoeic — a decisive, single motion.
3.22: wayyāṣēʾ happaršedōnāh — Ehud went out via happaršedōn. The word paršedōn is a hapax legomenon of contested meaning: 'the anteroom/vestibule,' or (following some interpreters) a term for the excretion caused by the wound. The double-hapax sequence (v.22-23) in a high-action scene may preserve archaic architectural vocabulary or reflect deliberate narrative distancing from the deed's gruesomeness through rare/opaque language.
3.23: wayyisgor daltôt hāʿăliyyāh baʿădô ûnāʿal — Ehud shut the roof-chamber doors behind him and locked them. The locked door is the mechanism that creates the servants' confusion and delay (v.24-25), enabling Ehud's escape. The narrative's precise attention to the door-locking detail anticipates its payoff in v.24.
3.24: ʾak mēsîk hûʾ ʾet-raglāyw baḥădar hammĕqērāh — 'surely he is relieving himself' (lit. 'covering his feet,' a euphemism for defecation). The servants' misreading of a locked door as their king on the toilet creates the time-delay that saves Ehud. The comic irony — Moabite servants imagining their king on the toilet while he lies dead — de-glamorizes royal pretension through scatalogical humor.
3.25: wayyāḥîlû ʿad-bôš — 'they waited until they were embarrassed' (bôš = shame, to be put to shame). The same verb describes the people's shame while Moses delayed on Sinai (Exod 32:1). The servants' shame-driven delay is Ehud's escape window. When propriety finally broke, they unlocked the doors with a key and found Eglon fallen dead on the floor.
3.26: wĕʾēhûd nimlāṭ bĕhitmāhĕmāham — 'Ehud escaped during their delay/hesitation.' The verb māhah (delay, tarry, hesitate) is the servants' fatal failure of initiative. He passes back through the pĕsilîm (idolatrous standing stones) on his escape route — the same contested spiritual geography he navigated on the way in.
3.27: wayyitqaʿ baššôpār bĕhar ʾeprayim wayyērĕdû ʾimmô bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl — 'he sounded the shofar in the hill country of Ephraim and the Israelites went down with him.' The šôpār (ram's horn trumpet) is the assembly signal (Judg 6:34; 1 Sam 13:3; Num 10:9) — here simultaneously announcing the oppressor's death and calling Israel's warriors. wĕhûʾ lipnêhem (with him at their head) — Ehud leads from the front.
3.28: kî nātan YHWH ʾet-ʾōyĕbêkem ʾet-môʾāb bĕyedkem — 'YHWH has given your enemies into your hand.' The proleptic perfect again: the victory declared complete before the decisive engagement. Ehud commands seizure of the Jordan fords (maʿabĕrôt hayyardēn lĕmôʾāb) to cut off Moabite retreat — tactical precision ensuring no escape. lōʾ nātĕnû ʾîš laʿăbōr — 'they let no man cross.'
3.29: wayyakkû ʾet-môʾāb baʿēt hahîʾ kaʿăśeret ʾălāpîm ʾîš kol-šāmēn wĕkol-ʾîš ḥāyil wĕlōʾ nimlāṭ ʾîš — 'they killed about ten thousand Moabites, all strong, all able-bodied men; not a man escaped.' Šāmēn (fat, well-fed) echoes bārîʾ for Eglon — the elite, prosperous Moabite military destroyed completely. Total victory (wĕlōʾ nimlāṭ ʾîš) signals full covenant restoration after oppression.
3.30: wayyikānaʿ môʾāb bayyôm hahûʾ taḥat yad yiśrāʾēl waytišqōṭ hāʾāreṣ šĕmōnîm šānāh — 'Moab was subdued; the land had rest eighty years.' Kānaʿ (be subdued, humbled) shares a root with Kĕnaʿan (Canaan) — the Canaanite subjugation vocabulary is applied to Moab. The eighty-year rest — double Othniel's period — perhaps reflects the completeness of this particular victory, or the proportionally longer recovery from the longer combined oppression (eight + eighteen = twenty-six years).
3.31: wayyak ʾet-pĕlištîm šēš-mēʾôt ʾîš bĕmalmad habbāqār — Shamgar killed six hundred Philistines with an oxgoad (malmad habbāqār). The malmad (pointed cattle-driving stick) is a farm implement, not a weapon — the Judges pattern of YHWH working through the unexpected means. gam-hûʾ hôšîaʿ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl — 'he also delivered Israel.' The brief formula confers full deliverer status on Shamgar despite a single verse. The name ben-ʿănāt (son of Anat) suggests non-Israelite background — another outsider incorporated into Israel's salvation history.

14.1: wayyēreḏ šimšôn timnātāh wayyarʾ ʾiššāh bətimnātāh mibənôt pəlištîm — 'Samson went down to Timnah and saw a woman in Timnah from the daughters of the Philistines.' yāraḏ (H3381, to go down) — Timnah (timnātāh H8553) lay in the Sorek Valley below the hill country of Dan, so 'going down' is geographically accurate. The woman is from the Philistines (pəlištîm H6430) — the dominant coastal people who oppressed Israel throughout Samson's era. Samson's entire career is conducted at the intersection of Israelite identity and Philistine territory; his marriages, riddles, and battles all occur on this borderland.
14.3: wayyōʾmer ləhî minnāh kî hîʾ yāšərāh bəʿênāy — 'Get her for me, for she is right in my eyes.' yāšar (H3474, to be straight/right/upright) + bəʿênāy (in my eyes) — the same phrase as Judges' refrain 'hayyāšār bəʿênāyw' (what is right in his own eyes, 17:6; 21:25). Samson's demand for this woman is the personal embodiment of the book's theological problem: Israel does what is right in its own eyes rather than in YHWH's eyes. His father objects on grounds of the ban against intermarriage (Deut 7:3); Samson overrides by asserting his own judgment. The narrator's aside in v4 — 'his father and mother did not know that it was from YHWH' — adds divine sovereignty: YHWH is working his purpose even through Samson's rebellious desire.
14.4: wəʾāḇîw wəʾimmô lōʾ yāḏəʿû kî mēYHWH hîʾ kî-ṯōʾănāh hûʾ məḇaqquēš miPəlištîm — 'His father and mother did not know that it was from YHWH, for he was seeking an occasion against the Philistines.' tōʾănāh (H8385, occasion/pretext/opportunity — from ʾānaʾ, to seek a pretext, only here) is a technical term for the spark needed to start a conflict. YHWH is sovereignly using Samson's personal desires to create the necessary confrontation with Philistia. The verse is a remarkable statement of divine providence operating through human passions — YHWH does not approve of the intermarriage, yet works through it to accomplish his deliverance purpose.
14.6: wattiṣlaḥ ʿālāyw rûaḥ YHWH wayyəšassəʿēhû kəšassaʿ haggerî wəʾumāh ʾên bəyāḏô — 'The Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and he tore it apart as one tears apart a young goat, and he had nothing in his hand.' ṣālaḥ (H6743, to rush/advance upon — used consistently for the Spirit's empowerment of the judges: 3:10 Othniel, 11:29 Jephthah, 14:19 Samson again; and 1 Sam 10:6,10; 16:13 for Saul and David) — the Spirit's arrival is sudden, powerful, and directive. šissēaʿ (H8156, to split/tear — the verb used for tearing apart a kid) applied to a lion carcass: Samson tears a lion bare-handed as though it were a kid. The 'nothing in his hand' detail is significant: this is pure Spirit-empowered strength, not a weapon feat.
14.8: wayyelek ləqaḥtāh wayyāsar lirʾôt ʾēt mappelet hāʾaryēh wəhinnēh ʿăḏat dəḇôrîm bəgəwiyyat hāʾaryēh ûḏəḇāš — 'He turned aside to see the carcass of the lion, and behold, there was a swarm of bees in the carcass of the lion, and honey.' mappelet (H4658, carcass/fallen thing — from nāpal to fall) — the lion's body. That bees have colonized it implies it has dried out (bees avoid rotting flesh). The honey in the lion's carcass is technically from a ṭāmēʾ (H2931, unclean) animal (Lev 11:27-28 — animals with paws are unclean; contact with their carcasses renders one unclean). Samson eats the honey without hesitation, compounding his Nazirite violations (no contact with corpses, Num 6:6). The honey becomes his riddle's answer: sweet from the strong.
14.12: wayyōʾmer lāhem šimšôn ʾāḥûḏāh-nāʾ lākem ḥîḏāh ʾim-hāḡēḏ taggîḏû ʾōtāh lî šiḇʿat yəmê hammišteh — 'Samson said to them: Let me now put a riddle to you. If you can explain it to me within the seven days of the feast...' ḥîḏāh (H2420, riddle/enigma/dark saying — from ḥûḏ H2330, to tie knots) is a wisdom genre: a knotted/twisted saying that conceals meaning. Riddles were a form of wisdom contest at banquets (1 Kgs 10:1 — Queen of Sheba tested Solomon with ḥîḏôt). Samson's riddle is personal-autobiographical: only he and YHWH know the experience behind it, making it technically unsolvable from public knowledge.
14.14: min-hāʾōkēl yāṣāʾ maʾăkāl ûmēʿaz yāṣāʾ māṯôq — 'Out of the eater came something to eat, and out of the strong came something sweet.' ʾōkēl (H398 participle, the eater — a lion) / maʾăkāl (H3978, food/something to eat — honey) / ʿaz (H5794, strong/fierce — the lion) / māṯôq (H4966, sweet — honey). The riddle is a verbal paradox built on unexpected reversals: the destroyer produces food; the fierce produces sweetness. The answer requires knowing the specific lion-and-honey experience. Without that knowledge, the Philistines can only solve it by extracting it from Samson's wife — which is exactly what they do, triggering the conflict YHWH intended (v4).
14.19: wattiṣlaḥ ʿālāyw rûaḥ YHWH wayyēreḏ ʾašqəlôn wayyak mēhem šəlōšîm ʾîš — 'The Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and he went down to Ashkelon and struck thirty men of them.' The Spirit's empowerment here enables Samson's 'payment' of the wager debt — thirty sets of garments taken from thirty Philistine dead. This is the Spirit being used for what looks like personal vengeance, but the narrative frames it as YHWH's larger campaign against Philistia. Ashkelon (ʾašqəlôn H831) was one of the five major Philistine cities (Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Gath, Ekron), demonstrating Samson's raids span the entire Philistine territory.
14.2: wayyaʿal wayyaggēḏ ləʾāḇîw ûləʾimmô wayyōʾmer ʾiššāh rāʾîtî lətimnātāh mibənôt pəlištîm wəʿattāh qəḥû-ʾōtāh lî ləʾiššāh — 'He came up and told his father and his mother: I saw a woman in Timnah, from the daughters of the Philistines. Now get her for me as a wife.' rāʾîtî (H7200, I saw) — sight triggers desire throughout Samson's story: he sees the Timnite woman (14:1), the Gaza prostitute (16:1), and Delilah (16:4). The pattern of desire-from-sight leading to disaster mirrors the Eden narrative (Gen 3:6) and foreshadows his literal blindness in 16:21 — Samson who lived by sight ends without eyes. qəḥû-ʾōtāh lî (get her for me) — the imperative to parents; he makes demands rather than requests, a pattern of self-will throughout.
14.5: wayyēreḏ šimšôn wəʾāḇîw wəʾimmô timnātāh wayyāḇōʾû ʾad-karmê timnāh wəhinnēh kəpîr ʾărāyôt šōʾēg liqrāʾtô — 'Samson went down with his father and mother to Timnah; when he came to the vineyards of Timnah, behold, a young lion came roaring toward him.' kəpîr ʾărāyôt (H3715 H738, young lion — a lion past the cub stage, in full strength) — the Timnah vineyards are themselves ironic setting: Samson the Nazirite (nāzîr, consecrated to abstain from grapes, Num 6:4) walks through a vineyard on his way to compound his violations. The lion ambush sets up the honey riddle and triggers the Spirit's first public display through Samson.
14.7: wayyēreḏ wayyədabbēr lāʾiššāh wattiîšar bəʿênê šimšôn — 'He went down and spoke with the woman, and she seemed right in Samson's eyes.' wattiîšar (H3474, she seemed right/good/pleasing) — the narrator repeats the root yāšar from v3: the woman who 'is right in my eyes' (v3) now 'seems right in his eyes' upon closer acquaintance. The repetition reinforces that Samson's evaluation is purely subjective and self-referential. He never learns her name; she exists in the narrative only in relation to him — a troubling pattern in the Samson cycle that reflects his inability to form covenantal relationships.
14.9: wayyirḏēhû ʾel-kappāyw wayyēlek hālôk wəʾākōl wayyēlek ʾel-ʾāḇîw wəʾel-ʾimmô wayyittēn lāhem wayyōʾkelû wəlōʾ higgîḏ lāhem kî mippegerhat hāʾaryēh rāḏāh hadəḇāš — 'He scraped the honey into his hands and went on, eating as he went. He came to his father and mother and gave some to them, and they ate. But he did not tell them that he had scraped the honey from the carcass of the lion.' rādāh (H7287/H7300, to scrape/draw out — the specific act of extracting honey from a comb) — Samson knows that touching the lion's carcass renders him and anyone who touches his food ritually unclean (Num 6:6-9). He conceals this from his parents, making them unknowing participants in defilement. The concealment is not caution but complicity: the Nazirite violates his vow and involves his family.
14.10: wayyēreḏ ʾāḇîw ʾel-hāʾiššāh wayyaʿaś šam šimšôn mišteh kî kēn yaʿăśû habbaḥûrîm — 'His father went down to the woman; Samson made a feast there, for so the young men used to do.' mišteh (H4960, feast/drinking feast — from šātāh H8354, to drink) — the feast was specifically a wine-drinking celebration, the standard wedding feast. That Samson the Nazirite makes a mišteh — a wine feast — is a direct Nazirite violation (Num 6:3: no wine or strong drink, no grape juice, no fresh or dried grapes). The narrator notes it was customary for young men, explaining why no one objects, but the violation stands: the consecrated man participates in what his vow forbids.
14.11: wayhî kirʾôtām ʾōtô wayyiqḥû šəlōšîm mēraʿîm wayyihyû ʾittô — 'When they saw him, they brought thirty companions to be with him.' mēraʿîm (H4828 plural, companions/attendants) — the thirty companions assigned by the Philistines may have been social custom or security measure. In the context of Samson's isolation (his parents returned home) and the Philistine setting, thirty men watching him functions as surveillance. The number thirty becomes the wager amount: 30 linen garments and 30 festal garments (v12-13) — if Samson loses the riddle, these thirty men profit. If he wins, they must pay. Their subsequent extortion of the answer through his wife reveals their intent from the start.
14.13: wəʾim-lōʾ tûkəlû ləhaggîḏ lî wənatatttem ʾattem lî šəlōšîm sādîn wəšəlōšîm ḥălipôt bəḡāḏîm — 'But if you cannot tell me, then you shall give me thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothes.' sādîn (H5466, linen garment/sheet — fine linen undergarment) and ḥălipôt bəḡāḏîm (H2487 H899, changes of clothing — festal outer garments) — the wager involves two complete sets of clothing for thirty men each. The total value is substantial: 60 quality garments. Samson is wagering on the unprovability of a private experience; the Philistines are gambling that social pressure on his wife will extract the answer — which is exactly what they proceed to do.
14.15: wayhî bayyôm haššəḇîʿî wayyōʾmərû ləʾēšet šimšôn pittî ʾet-ʾîšēk wəyaggēḏ-lānû ʾet-haḥîḏāh — 'On the seventh day they said to Samson's wife: Entice your husband, that he may tell us the riddle.' pittāh (H6601, to entice/seduce/deceive — the same verb used of false prophets in Ezek 14:9 and the seduction of a virgin in Exod 22:16) — the verb is strong: they are asking the woman to use seduction and manipulation to extract intelligence. pen-niśrəpāh ʾôtāk wə-ʾet-bêt ʾāḇîk bāʾēš — 'lest we burn you and your father's house with fire' — the threat is lethal and immediate; the woman is trapped between her husband and her people's threats.
14.16: wattibekkeh ʾēšet šimšôn ʿālāyw wattoʾmer śānēʾtanî wəlōʾ ʾăhaḇtānî — 'Samson's wife wept over him and said: You only hate me; you do not love me.' śānēʾ (H8130, to hate — the antonym of covenant love ʾāhab) and ʾāhab (H157, to love) — the wife frames the riddle secret as a test of marital love. Her accusation reframes an intelligence extraction as an emotional appeal: if you loved me, you'd tell me. haḥîḏāh ḥaḏtāh wəlōʾ higgadtāh lî — 'you have put a riddle to my countrymen and have not told me.' The irony is that she is being coerced by her kinsmen to extract what she frames as a matter of love — her tears and accusations work on Samson's affections exactly as the Philistines calculated.
14.17: wattibekkeh ʿālāyw šiḇʿat hayyāmîm ʾăšer-hāyāh lāhem hammišteh wayhî bayyôm haššəḇîʿî wayyaggēḏ-lāh kî-hēṣîqat-lô — 'She wept before him the seven days that their feast lasted, and on the seventh day he told her because she pressed him hard.' hēṣîq (H6693, to press hard/distress — same root as 16:16 where Delilah presses Samson daily until his soul was shortened to death) — the verb anticipates the Delilah pattern: a woman's persistent emotional pressure extracts Samson's secret. He is vulnerable to relational pressure from women in a way he is not vulnerable to physical force. The seven-day persistence is successful: love conquers the riddle's security.
14.18: wayyōʾmərû lô ʾanšê hāʿîr bayyôm haššəḇîʿî bəṭerem yiššaqep haḥammāh mah-māṯôq midəḇāš ûmah-ʿaz mēʾaryēh — 'The men of the city said to him on the seventh day before the sun went down: What is sweeter than honey? What is stronger than a lion?' Samson's devastating response: lûlēʾ ḥărašttem bəʿeglātî lōʾ məṣāʾtem ḥîḏātî — 'If you had not plowed with my heifer, you would not have found out my riddle.' ḥāraš (H2790, to plow/engrave) + ʿeglāh (H5697, heifer) — the metaphor is agricultural and sexual: 'plowing with my heifer' = using my wife for your purposes. The image of a wife as a working animal is harsh and reflects Samson's objectifying perspective; yet it accurately captures what the Philistines did — they used her as a tool to extract information.
14.20: wattihyeh ʾēšet šimšôn ləmērēʿēhû ʾăšer rēʿāh-lô — 'And Samson's wife was given to his companion, who had been his best man.' mērēʿ (H4828, companion/friend — the companion who served as his best man at the wedding, called his mērēʿ) — the wife is transferred to the wedding companion when Samson abandons her in anger. This completes the Timnah episode's arc: Samson went down seeking a wife and returns without one. The transfer becomes the pretext for the fox-and-fire campaign (15:1-5). Every action in the Samson cycle sets up the next escalation; nothing is resolved cleanly because Samson acts from anger rather than covenant faithfulness.

15.4: wayyēlek šimšôn wayyilkoḏ šəlōš-mêʾôt šûʿālîm wayyiqqaḥ lappîḏîm wayyipeŋ zānāḇ ʾel-zānāḇ — 'Samson went and caught three hundred foxes and took torches; he turned tail to tail and put one torch between each pair of tails.' šûʿāl (H7776, fox/jackal — both animals are intended by the word) — the catching of 300 animals is itself a Samson feat. lappîḏ (H3940, torch/firebrand — the same word as in Judg 7:16 where Gideon's men carry torches) — firebrands between paired fox tails would have created uncontrollable running fire through standing grain. The agricultural sabotage destroys the Philistine harvest at precisely the wrong time — vindictive, effective, and proportionally catastrophic.
15.11: wayyōʾmərû lô maddûaʿ ʿālîtā ʿālênû wayyōʾmer lāhem kaʾăšer ʿāśû lî kēn ʿāśîtî lāhem — 'They said to him: Do you not know that the Philistines rule over us? What is this that you have done to us? He said: As they did to me, so I have done to them.' māšal (H4910, to rule/have dominion) — the Philistines 'rule over' Israel; Israel has internalized the oppressor's authority as normal and resents any disruption to it. Samson's 'measure for measure' (kaʾăšer... kēn) ethic is lex talionis (Lev 24:19-20) — exact reciprocity. But the real irony is that Israel ties up their own deliverer to hand him to the Philistines (vv12-13).
15.14: wattiṣlaḥ ʿālāyw rûaḥ YHWH wattihyênāh hāʾăḇôtîm ʾăšer ʿal-zərōʿāyw kappîštîm ʾăšer bāʿărû ḇāʾēš — 'The Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and the ropes on his arms became like flax burned with fire.' For the third time in Samson's story ṣālaḥ rûaḥ YHWH — the Spirit rushes upon him. pišṭāh (H6593, flax/linen thread) + bāʿar ḇāʾēš (burning with fire) — the ropes that would bind him dissolve like burning flax. The metaphor captures instantaneous dissolution: fire makes flax linen structurally worthless immediately. This third Spirit-empowerment in ch14-15 establishes the pattern that will be broken in ch16.
15.15: wayyimṣāʾ lḥî-ḥāmôr ṭəriyyāh wayyišlaḥ yāḏô wayyiqqāḥēhû wayyak-bô ʾelep ʾîš — 'He found a fresh jawbone of a donkey, reached out and took it, and struck down a thousand men with it.' ləḥî-ḥāmôr (H3895 H2543, jaw/cheek of a donkey) — the jawbone weapon is deliberately humble: not a sword, spear, or club but an agricultural animal's discarded bone. ṭəriyyāh (H2961, fresh/new — not yet dried and brittle) — the moisture in a fresh jawbone gives it structural integrity as a weapon. The contrast with conventional weapons echoes the theological principle of Judges: YHWH delivers through inadequate means (tent peg, jawbone) to make his exclusive agency clear.
15.18: wayyiṣmāʾ məʾōḏ wayyiqrāʾ ʾel-YHWH wayyōʾmer ʾattāh nātattā bəyaḏ-ʿaḇdəkā ʾet-hayyəšûʿāh hagdôlāh hazzōʾt — 'He was very thirsty, and he called to YHWH and said: You have given this great salvation by the hand of your servant.' Samson prays — one of the rare moments of direct divine address in his story. yəšûʿāh gəḏôlāh (H3444 H1419, great deliverance/salvation) — the same vocabulary as YHWH's covenant saving acts throughout the OT. Even Samson, who often acts independently, must acknowledge that the victory was YHWH's gift. waʾattāh ʾāmût baṣṣāmāʾ — 'and now shall I die of thirst?' — after the great victory, ordinary thirst threatens the life YHWH just used for deliverance. The prayer is answered immediately (v19).
15.19: wayyibqaʿ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-hammakteš ʾăšer ballĕḥî wayyēṣəʾû mimmennû mayim wayyišt wayyāšob rûḥô wayyiḥî — 'God split open the hollow place that is in Lehi, and water came out from it, and he drank, and his spirit returned and he revived.' bāqaʿ (H1234, to split/break open — used of the Exodus sea, Ps 78:13; of the rock in the wilderness, Ps 78:15-16) — God provides water in the wilderness pattern; the jawbone victory is followed by a water-from-rock moment. makteš (H4388, hollow/mortar — the place of grinding, also a bowl-shaped depression in rock) becomes the spring. The place is named ʿên haqqôrēʾ (H5879, 'Spring of the One Who Calls/Called') — a place-name encoding Samson's prayer, preserved in geography as testimony to answered prayer.
15.1: wayhî miyāmîm biqṣîr-ḥiṭṭîm wayyiqqōḏ šimšôn ʾet-ʾištô bigəḏî-ʿizzîm wayyōʾmer ʾābōʾāh ʾel-ʾištî haḥāḏərāh — 'After some time, at the time of wheat harvest, Samson visited his wife with a young goat, and he said: I will go into my wife in the chamber.' The wheat harvest timing (qəṣîr-ḥiṭṭîm) places this return in late spring. gəḏî-ʿizzîm (H1423 H5795, young goat) — a young goat was a standard reconciliation/visit gift in ancient Near Eastern custom (Gen 38:17 Judah to Tamar; Gen 38:20). Samson returns expecting to resume the marriage, apparently unaware that his wife has been given away. The goat gift implies he is making a goodwill approach, not coming in anger — which makes the father's disclosure in v2 even more devastating.
15.2: wayyōʾmer ʾāḇîhā ʾāmōr ʾāmartî kî-śānōʾ śənēʾtāh — 'But her father said: I really thought that you utterly hated her.' śānōʾ śənēʾtāh (infinitive absolute + finite verb — the intensified form: 'utterly hated') — the father explains his decision: Samson's angry departure after the wedding feast (14:19) was read as marital abandonment, and under ancient Near Eastern custom an abandoned wife could be given to another. wāʾettənāh ləmērēʿăkā hălōʾ ʾăḥōtāh haqqəṭannāh ṭôḇāh mimmenāh — 'so I gave her to your companion. Is not her younger sister more beautiful than she? Please take her instead.' The offer of the younger sister is a face-saving consolation prize; Samson's response rejects it and frames his response as righteous retaliation.
15.3: wayyōʾmer lāhem šimšôn niqqêtî happaʿam mippəlištîm kî-ʾōśeh ʾănî ʿimmāhem rāʿāh — 'Samson said to them: This time I shall be blameless with regard to the Philistines, when I do them harm.' niqqêtî (H5352 Niphal, to be blameless/innocent/acquitted — from nāqāh H5352) — Samson declares his retaliatory action to be morally justified: he is making himself 'clean' or 'acquitted' by responding in kind. The logic is lex talionis (they wronged him; he is entitled to respond). Whether the declaration is theologically legitimate is ambiguous — YHWH does not comment. The narrative uses Samson's personal grievances as the vehicle for national-scale Philistine punishment, but Samson's framing is entirely self-referential.
15.5: wayyabbeʿ-ʾēš balappîḏîm wayšallaḥ bəqāmôt pəlištîm wayyabʿar miggādîš wəʿaḏ-qāmāh wəʿaḏ-kerem zayit — 'He lit the torches and set the foxes free into the standing grain of the Philistines, and burned up the shocks and the standing grain and also the olive orchards.' gādîš (H1430, shock of grain — grain already cut and stacked for drying) and qāmāh (H7054, standing grain — still unharvested) and kerem zayit (H3754 H2132, olive orchard) — three stages of agricultural production are destroyed: stored grain, unharvested grain, and the perennial orchards that take decades to mature. The olive trees are the most severe loss: destroying an olive grove sets back a community's food supply for a generation. This is total agricultural warfare.
15.6: wayyōʾmərû pəlištîm mî ʿāśāh zōʾt wayyōʾmərû šimšôn ḥătan hattimānî kî lāqaḥ ʾet-ʾištô wayyittnāh ləmērēʿēhû wayyaʿălû pəlištîm wayyiśrəpû ʾôtāh wəʾet-ʾāḇîhā bāʾēš — 'The Philistines said: Who has done this? They said: Samson, the son-in-law of the Timnite, because he took his wife and gave her to his companion. And the Philistines came up and burned her and her father with fire.' The Philistines retaliate against the woman and her father for Samson's action — punishing the innocent parties rather than Samson himself. The burning of the woman (the very threat made in 14:15 to extract the riddle) comes full circle: the threat used to coerce her is now executed against her. The cycle of vengeance expands: Philistines burned Samson → Samson burned their fields → Philistines burned his wife → Samson strikes them again (v7-8).
15.7: wayyōʾmer lāhem šimšôn ʾim-tāʿăśûn kāzōʾt kî ʾim-niqqamtî bākem wəʾaḥar ʾeḥdāl — 'Samson said to them: If you do this, I will surely take revenge on you, and after that I will stop.' niqqam (H5358, to avenge/take revenge — the same root as Samson's final prayer in 16:28) — Samson's life is structured as a cycle of revenge declarations followed by devastating acts of force. Each declaration escalates the conflict. The phrase wəʾaḥar ʾeḥdāl — 'and after that I will stop' — is Samson's pattern of self-imposed limits that he never actually keeps; the conflict always continues because his actions generate new retaliation.
15.8: wayyak ʾôtām šôq ʿal-yārēk makkāh gəḏôlāh wayyēreḏ wayyēšeḇ bəsəʿîp-sela haʿêṭām — 'He struck them hip and thigh with a great blow, and he went down and stayed in the cleft of the rock of Etam.' šôq ʿal-yārēk (H7785 H3409, shin/thigh upon thigh — an idiom of uncertain origin, possibly from wrestling or battle, describing a thorough, total defeat: 'piling blow on blow') — 'hip and thigh' is a unique idiomatic expression meaning a comprehensive, unreserved slaughter. Samson then withdraws to the rock of Etam (sela haʿêṭām), a natural fortress in Judah's territory — the pattern of retreat after a major act of force that leaves the political problem unresolved.
