15.9: wayyaʿălû pəlištîm wayyaḥănû bîhûḏāh wayyinnāṭəšû bəlaḥî — 'Then the Philistines came up and encamped in Judah and made a raid on Lehi.' nāṭaš (H5203, to spread out/deploy/raid — used of armies spreading across terrain) — the Philistine incursion into Judah proper over a single man demonstrates both Samson's military significance to them and Judah's political vulnerability. laḥî (H3896, Lehi — meaning 'jawbone') is the place name that will receive its etiology from the upcoming battle (v9-17). The geographic specificity grounds the narrative: this is territory history, not legend.
15.10: wayyōʾmərû ʾanšê yəhûḏāh lāmāh ʿălîtem ʿālênû wayyōʾmərû laʾăsōr ʾet-šimšôn ʿālînû — 'The men of Judah said: Why have you come up against us? They said: We have come up to bind Samson, to do to him as he did to us.' The Philistines' answer is specific: they want Samson, not territory. The men of Judah's compliance in v12-13 — binding their own deliverer to hand him to the enemy — is one of Judges' most disturbing moments: Israel has internalized subjugation so thoroughly that they participate in suppressing their own liberation. The oppressor controls them not just by force but by persuading them to police themselves.
15.12: wayyōʾmərû lô laʾăsōr-kā yāraḏnû lōsōr-kā ûlitttənkā bəyaḏ-pəlištîm wayyōʾmer lāhem šimšôn hišāḇəʿû lî pen-tipgəʿûn-bî ʾattem — 'They said to him: We have come down to bind you, that we may give you into the hands of the Philistines. And Samson said to them: Swear to me that you will not attack me yourselves.' Samson's one condition — that his own people not strike him — reveals his trust calculus: he trusts Judah's oath more than the Philistines' restraint. hišāḇəʿû (H7650 Hiphil, to cause to swear) — Samson administers an oath to his own countrymen before surrendering to them. The scene is a grotesque inversion of military protocol: a deliverer being bound and surrendered by the people he came to deliver.
15.13: wayyōʾmərû lô lēʾmōr lōʾ ʾim-ʾāsōr neʾesārkā ûnətatnākā bəyāḏām wəhārōg lōʾ nahargekkā wayyaʾasrūhû bišənê ʿăbōtîm ḥăḏāšîm wayyaʿăluhû min-hassālaʿ — 'They said to him: No; we will only bind you and give you into their hand. We will not kill you. So they bound him with two new ropes and brought him up from the rock.' ʿăbōt ḥăḏāšîm (H5688 H2319, new ropes) — the 'new ropes' detail anticipates ch16:11 where the same phrase appears in Delilah's second trap. The two new ropes that bind Samson here will dissolve like flax (v14) — the same pattern established in ch16 in reverse. Judah binds their deliverer with new ropes; the Spirit dissolves them. YHWH overrules Israel's complicity.
15.16: wayyōʾmer šimšôn bilḥî haḥāmôr ḥămôr ḥămōrātayim bilḥî haḥāmôr hikkêtî ʾelep ʾîš — 'Samson said: With the jawbone of a donkey, heaps upon heaps, with the jawbone of a donkey I have struck down a thousand men.' bilḥî haḥāmôr (with the jawbone of the donkey) — the victory song is a wordplay: ḥāmôr (H2543, donkey) sounds like ḥōmer (H2563, heap/pile of grain). ḥămôr ḥămōrātayim may be read as 'heaps, two heaps' — a doubling that intensifies the count. The boast song is Samson's only recorded speech-act of praise, and its grammar is military: first-person victorious declaration. Compare Deborah's song (ch5): Samson's victory song is self-referential where Deborah's is YHWH-referential.
15.17: wayhî kəkallōtô lədabbēr wayyašleḵ haləḥî miyyāḏô wayyiqrāʾ lamāqôm hahûʾ rāmat-laḥî — 'When he had finished speaking, he threw away the jawbone, and that place was called Ramath-lehi.' rāmat-laḥî (H7437, 'height of the jawbone' or 'throwing of the jawbone') — the place name encodes the weapon's use. In Judges, place-naming is a consistent narrative practice marking sites of divine intervention: Beer-lahai-roi (Gen 16:14), Penuel (Gen 32:31), Bochim (Judg 2:5), and here. The physical jawbone becomes a geographic marker of YHWH's deliverance, preserved in the landscape as testimony.
15.20: wayyišpōṭ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl bîmê pəlištîm ʿeśrîm šānāh — 'And he judged Israel in the days of the Philistines twenty years.' The twenty-year judgeship formula (ʿeśrîm šānāh) closes the Samson cycle's second act (cf. 16:31 where the same formula closes his career). The formula is deliberately minimal compared to other judges: no 'the land had rest for X years,' no summary of peace. The phrasing 'in the days of the Philistines' acknowledges that Samson's judgeship did not end Philistine domination — unlike Othniel, Deborah, or Gideon, Samson did not liberate the land. He harassed the Philistines without resolving the crisis, leaving the work for Samuel and David.

16.4: wayyelek wayyeʾĕhab ʾiššāh binḥal śôrēq ûšəmāh dəlîlāh — 'He went and loved a woman in the Valley of Sorek whose name was Delilah.' ʾāhab (H157, to love — used of covenant/romantic love; cf. Deut 6:5 loving YHWH) — Samson 'loved' Delilah with the same verb as covenant-love; the tragic irony is that she will betray this love for money. dəlîlāh (H1807) — her name may derive from dālal (H1809, to dangle/hang low, to be brought low) or from dālāh (to draw water). The Sorek Valley (naḥal śôrēq H8256) runs from the Judean hills to the Mediterranean — deep in borderland territory between Israel and Philistia.
16.6: wattoʾmer dəlîlāh ʾel-šimšôn haggîḏāh-nāʾ lî bammāh kōḥăkā gāḏôl — 'Delilah said to Samson: Please tell me where your great strength lies.' kōaḥ (H3581, strength/power/force) is the key word of ch16 — it appears in Delilah's question (v6), her repeated interrogation (vv9,15), and in Samson's final prayer ('strengthen me just this once,' v28). The ultimate source of Samson's strength is not physical but spiritual — the Nazirite consecration that made him YHWH's separated person. Samson initially lies (vv7,11,13) — he knows the truth is spiritually dangerous, but the relationship pressure gradually erodes his resistance.
16.17: wayyaggēḏ-lāh ʾet-kol-libbô wayyōʾmer lāh môrāh lōʾ-ʿālāh ʿal-rōʾšî kî-nəzîr ʾĕlōhîm ʾānî mibbeṭen ʾimmî — 'He told her all his heart and said to her: A razor has never come upon my head, for I have been a Nazirite to God from my mother's womb.' kol-libbô (H3820, all his heart) — the full disclosure: Samson finally tells the whole truth. môrāh (H4177, razor — from mûr to change, or possibly from yārēʾ to fear) — the razor is what has never touched his head. nəzîr ʾĕlōhîm (H5139, Nazirite of God) — the Nazirite vow (Num 6:1-21) involved three separations: no grape products, no razor, no contact with corpses. Samson has violated the first and third throughout his story; the hair alone remains. The disclosure is suicide by confession.
16.19: wattiśan ʾet-šiḇʿat maḥlĕpôt rōʾšô wayttəḥel ləʿannôṯô — 'She shaved off the seven locks of his head.' maḥlāpôt (H4253, locks/braids of hair — from ḥālap H2498, to pass through/change) — the seven locks (šebaʿ maḥlāpôt) correspond to seven units of consecrated growth: the Nazirite hair was never cut and represented the accumulated time of YHWH's claim on the person. ʿinnāh (H6031, to afflict/humble/weaken) — with the shaving she 'humbles/afflicts' him; his strength departs. The verb ʿinnāh is also the word for the affliction of the oppressed — Samson now becomes the afflicted rather than the deliverer.
16.20: wəhûʾ lōʾ yāḏaʿ kî YHWH sār mēʿālāyw — 'But he did not know that YHWH had turned away from him.' The most tragic statement in the Samson narrative. sār (H5493 sûr, to turn aside/depart) — YHWH's presence-departure is the reversal of the commissioning promises ('YHWH is with you'). The fact that Samson 'does not know' (H3045 yāḏaʿ) is the spiritual tragedy: he still expects his strength to function as before (v20b — 'I will go out as before and shake myself free'). The assumption that YHWH's enabling presence continues even after covenant violation is the theological danger that Samson embodies — presuming on grace while abandoning its conditions.
16.22: wayyāḥel śəʿar-rōʾšô litzammēaḥ kaʾăšer gullāḥ — 'But the hair of his head began to grow again after it had been shaved.' The regrowth of Samson's hair is quiet, undramatic, and not commented on until this verse — signaling that YHWH's plan has not ended. gālaḥ (H1548, to shave — the same verb used in v19) / ṣāmaḥ (H6779, to grow/sprout) — the sprouting is the same verb used for vegetation growth, new shoots. The new hair represents new consecration: not the old Nazirite vow (which cannot be restored once broken, Num 6:12) but YHWH's sovereign re-empowerment for the final act.
16.28: wayyiqrāʾ šimšôn ʾel-YHWH wayyōʾmer ʾăḏōnāy YHWH zākərēnî nāʾ wəḥazzəqēnî nāʾ ʾaḵ happaʿam hazzeh hāʾĕlōhîm wəʾiqqāməh niqmat-ʾaḥat miššətê ʿênāy mippəlištîm — 'Samson called to YHWH and said: Lord YHWH, remember me please, and please strengthen me just this once, O God, that I may be avenged on the Philistines for one of my two eyes.' zākar (H2142, remember) — the covenant memory-language: YHWH remembering is his acting on prior commitment (cf. Gen 8:1; Exod 2:24). ḥāzaq (H2388, to be strong/strengthen — in Piel: to strengthen someone) — the prayer is for restored strength. nāqam (H5358, to avenge/take vengeance) — Samson's prayer frames the final act as personal revenge for his eyes rather than covenant deliverance for Israel; yet the narrator's assessment in v28b notes he killed more in his death than in his life.
16.30: wayyōʾmer šimšôn tāmôt napšî ʿim-pəlištîm wayyēṭ bəkōaḥ — 'Samson said: Let me die with the Philistines! And he bowed with all his might.' napšî (H5315, my soul/life/self) — his self-dedication to death. kōaḥ (H3581, might/strength) — the word that dominated the chapter returns for its final use: Samson exerts all his restored strength. The temple of Dagon (hêkal dāgôn, H1712) collapses — Dagon was the principal grain deity of the Philistines (possibly connected to dāgān H1715, grain). The destruction of the deity's house while 3,000 worshipers are celebrating victory over YHWH's champion is an implicit defeat of the deity itself. raḇ hammetîm ʾăšer-hēmît bəmôtô miʾăšer hēmît bəḥayyāyw — 'the dead whom he killed at his death were more than those he had killed during his life' — the tragic calculus of a flawed champion's greatest victory.
16.1: wayyēlek šimšôn ʿazzātāh wayyarʾ-šam ʾiššāh zônāh wayyāḇôʾ ʾēlêhā — 'Samson went to Gaza and saw there a prostitute, and he went in to her.' The Samson cycle's third woman follows the pattern: Samson sees (rāʾāh) and goes in. Gaza (ʿazzāh H5804) was one of the five Philistine capital cities — the deepest Samson has gone into enemy territory. zônāh (H2181, prostitute — from zānāh, to commit fornication/whoredom; the same root used metaphorically for Israel's spiritual adultery throughout Judges) — Samson the Nazirite in a Philistine city with a prostitute is the nadir of his Nazirite faithfulness before ch16's climax.
16.2: laʿazzātîm lēʾmōr bāʾ šimšôn hēnnāh wayyāsōbbû wayyeʾerəbû-lô kol-hallayla bəšaʿar hāʿîr — 'The Gazites were told: Samson has come here. And they surrounded the place and waited all night at the gate of the city. They kept quiet all night, saying: Let us wait till the light of morning; then we will kill him.' wayyeʾerəbû (H693, to lie in wait/ambush) — Gaza sets an ambush, keeping quiet through the night. The gate of the city (šaʿar hāʿîr) was the city's strongest defensive feature, controlling entrance and exit. The Gazites' patience and planning — waiting till morning light — contrasts with Samson's casual night visit; they are hunters setting a trap.
16.3: wayyiškaḇ šimšôn ʿad-ḥăṣôt hallaylāh wayyāqom baḥăṣôt hallaylāh wayyeʾĕḥōz bedaltôt šaʿar-hāʿîr ûbišnê hammezûzôt wayyisśāʾēm ʿim-habərîaḥ wayyāśem ʿal-kətēpāyw wayyaʿălēm ʾel-rōʾš hāhār ʾăšer ʿal-pənê ḥeḇrôn — 'Samson lay until midnight, and at midnight he arose and took hold of the doors of the gate of the city and the two posts, and pulled them up, bar and all, and put them on his shoulders and carried them to the top of the hill that is in front of Hebron.' The midnight escape with the city gate is Samson at his most audacious: a city gate with its posts and bar weighed several tons. ḥeḇrôn (H2275, Hebron) is approximately 40 miles from Gaza and sits on a high ridge — the carrying of Gaza's gate to Hebron's hill is a symbolic emasculation of Philistine power: their city's greatest defensive barrier removed and deposited in Israelite territory.
16.5: wayyaʿălû ʾēlêhā sarnê pəlištîm wayyōʾmərû lāh pittî ʾôtô ûrəʾî bammāh kōḥô gāḏôl ûbammāh nûkal lô waʾăsarnûhû ləʿannōtô wəʾănaḥnû nittēn-lāk ʾîš ʾelep ûmēʾāh kāsep — 'The lords of the Philistines came up to her and said to her: Entice him, and see where his great strength lies, and by what means we may overpower him, that we may bind him to humble him. And we will each give you 1100 pieces of silver.' sarnê pəlištîm (H5633, lords of the Philistines — the five sərānîm, one from each city: Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Gath, Ekron) — all five lords are involved; this is a national-level operation. 1100 × 5 = 5,500 pieces of silver is an enormous sum. pittî (H6601, entice/seduce) — the same verb used of enticing Samson's wife in 14:15. The Philistines know what worked once.
16.7: wayyōʾmer ʾēlêhā šimšôn ʾim-yāsərûnî bəšiḇʿāh yətārôt laḥôt ʾăšer lōʾ-yāḇəšû wəḥālîtî wəhāyîtî kəʾaḥaḏ hāʾāḏām — 'Samson said to her: If they bind me with seven fresh bowstrings that have not been dried, I shall become weak and be like any other man.' yetār laḥ (H3499 H3892, fresh bowstring/sinew — from a freshly slaughtered animal, still moist) — fresh sinew retains its elasticity; it was used for bow strings when still moist. The 'seven' (šiḇʿāh) is the number of completeness. Samson's first deception locates his power in material: physical binding. This misdirection will be repeated twice more, each time moving closer to the real answer — the hair and the consecration it represents.
16.8: wayyaʿălû-lāh sarnê pəlištîm šiḇʿāh yətārôt laḥôt ʾăšer lōʾ-yāḇəšû wattaʾasrēhû bāhem — 'The lords of the Philistines brought up to her seven fresh bowstrings that had not been dried, and she bound him with them.' Delilah actively binds Samson — in their own bed, evidently, since the ambushers are 'in the inner room' (v9). The extraordinary intimacy of the trap: Samson allows himself to be bound by someone he loves, in a private domestic setting, with enemies waiting in the next room. His trust is so complete that he does not perceive the danger even though the scenario should be obvious. The Philistine lords fund the operation and supply materials but Delilah does the actual binding.
16.9: wəhāʾōrēb yōšēḇ lāh bāḥāḏer wattoʾmer ʾēlāyw pəlištîm ʿālêkā šimšôn wayyənatteq ʾet-hayyətārôt kaʾăšer yinnātēq pətîl-hanuʿōret bəharîḥô ʾēš — 'The ambushers were sitting in her inner room; she said to him: The Philistines are upon you, Samson! But he snapped the bowstrings, as a thread of tow snaps when it touches the fire.' hāʾōrēb (H693, ambushers/liers in wait) — the domestic ambush is a perversion of the marriage bed: the bedroom becomes a trap. Samson's destruction of the bowstrings is instant and effortless, like burning thread. pətîl-hanuʿōret (H6306, a thread/cord of tow — the coarse, easily ignited fiber of flax) is the simile for how quickly the bowstrings dissolved. The deception is exposed but Samson does not flee or break off the relationship.
16.10: wattoʾmer dəlîlāh ʾel-šimšôn hinnēh hēteltatnî watədabbēr ʾēlay kəzāḇîm — 'Delilah said to Samson: Behold, you have mocked me and told me lies. Please tell me by what means you might be bound.' hētel (H2048, to mock/deceive — from tālaʿ, to deceive; rare term) — Delilah accuses Samson of lying to her, framing her surveillance as a matter of relational integrity. The accusation is audacious: she who is gathering intelligence for his enemies accuses him of dishonesty. Yet Samson accepts the framing and answers again. His willingness to continue engaging with the question — even after the ambush attempted to kill him — reveals his fatal attachment to Delilah despite all evidence.
16.11: wayyōʾmer ʾēlêhā ʾim-ʾāsōr yaʾasrûnî baʿăḇōtîm ḥăḏāšîm ʾăšer lōʾ-naʿăśāh bāhem məlāʾkāh wəḥālîtî wəhāyîtî kəʾaḥaḏ hāʾāḏām — 'He said to her: If they bind me with new ropes that have not been used, I shall become weak and be like any other man.' ʿăḇōtîm ḥăḏāšîm (H5688 H2319, new ropes) — the same ropes that bound Samson in 15:13 and were dissolved by the Spirit. The second deception moves from organic material (sinew) to manufactured rope — Samson is moving the answer systematically away from the truth (his hair/vow) by offering plausible-sounding alternatives. Yet each failure should teach Delilah something, and it does: the ropes are as ineffective as the bowstrings.
16.12: wattaʾasrēhû dəlîlāh baʿăḇōtîm ḥăḏāšîm wattōʾmer ʾēlāyw pəlištîm ʿālêkā šimšôn wəhāʾōrēb yōšēḇ bāḥāḏer wayyənatqēm mēʿal zərōʿōṯāyw kaḥûṭ — 'Delilah bound him with new ropes and said to him: The Philistines are upon you, Samson! And the ambushers were sitting in the inner room. But he snapped them off his arms like a thread.' The ropes snap 'like a thread' (kaḥûṭ H2339) — even finer than the previous simile. The escalating ease of escape signals that the problem is not the type of binding material but the source of Samson's power. Each failed attempt is narrowing the investigative space toward the real answer: his consecration, not his muscles.
16.13: wattoʾmer dəlîlāh ʾel-šimšôn ʿad-hēnnāh hētaltatnî wattədabbēr ʾēlay kəzāḇîm haggîḏāh-llî bammāh tuʾāsar — 'Delilah said to Samson: Until now you have mocked me and told me lies. Tell me by what you might be bound.' The third accusation of lying uses the same formula as the second (v10): hētel + kəzāḇîm. The repetition of the accusation structure reveals Delilah's methodology: emotional pressure, accusation of dishonesty, renewed request. Each cycle of accusation is slightly more intense. Samson is trapped between knowing he shouldn't answer and being unable to withstand the relational pressure she applies.
16.14: wattitqaʿ bayyātēd wattōʾmer ʾēlāyw pəlištîm ʿālêkā šimšôn wayyîqaṣ miššənātô wayyissaʿ ʾet-hayyātēḏ ʾet-haʾāreg wəʾet-hamannôr — 'She pinned it with the pin and said to him: Philistines are upon you, Samson! But he awoke from his sleep and pulled away the pin, the loom, and the web.' The third deception involves weaving Samson's hair into a loom's fabric — Delilah has moved to the hair itself, recognizing from Samson's evasions that the hair is involved. yātēḏ (H3489, pin/peg — the weaving pin) — she pins the woven web containing his hair to the loom. He tears it all away while asleep — yet still does not understand or refuse to return. The hair has been touched and he felt nothing: the Nazirite vow is more about consecration than the physical hair alone.
16.15: wattoʾmer ʾēlāyw ʾêk tōʾmar ʾăhaḇtîkā wəlibbəkā ʾên ʾittî — 'She said to him: How can you say: I love you, when your heart is not with me?' ʾāhaḇtîkā (H157, I love you) — Delilah weaponizes Samson's love declaration: if you love me, you'll tell me. wəlibbəkā ʾên ʾittî — 'your heart is not with me' — the accusation reaches deeper than dishonesty; she claims he is withholding himself, keeping a secret that proves the love is partial. The accusation resonates because it contains a truth: there is something Samson has not told her. Her reading is correct even though her purpose is predatory.
16.16: wayhî kî-hēṣîqāh-lô biddəḇārêhā kol-hayyāmîm wattəʾallāṣēhû wayyiqṣar napšô lamût — 'When she pressed him hard with her words day after day, and urged him, his soul was shortened to the point of death.' hēṣîq (H6693, to press hard/distress — identical verb as 14:17 where Samson's wife pressed him during the seven-day feast) — the pattern is deliberate: Samson's first wife pressed him for seven days to extract the riddle; Delilah presses him daily until his soul collapses. wayyiqṣar napšô lamût (his soul/life was shortened to death) — qāṣar nepeš is a Hebrew idiom for reaching the breaking point of endurance (cf. Num 21:4; Zech 11:8). Samson's will collapses not under physical threat but under relentless relational attrition.
16.18: wattirrəʾ dəlîlāh kî-higgîḏ lāh ʾet-kol-libbô wattiššlaḥ wattiqrāʾ lisarnê pəlištîm lēʾmōr ʿălû happaʿam hazzeh kî higgîḏ lî ʾet-kol-libbô — 'When Delilah saw that he had told her all his heart, she sent and called the lords of the Philistines, saying: Come up once more, for he has told me all his heart.' wattirrəʾ (H7200, and she saw) — Delilah perceives the truth of the disclosure: she recognizes sincerity where the previous three answers were evasions. How? The previous answers were testable and failed the test; this answer she verifies differently — perhaps through Samson's emotional vulnerability, perhaps through the specificity of 'from my mother's womb.' The Philistine lords come immediately, bringing the 1100 silver (v18b).
16.21: wayyōʾḥăzûhû pəlištîm wayyənaqərû ʾet-ʿênāyw wayyôrîḏûhû ʿazzātāh wayyaʾasrûhû bənḥušताyim wayhî ṭôḥên baḇêt hāʾăsûrîm — 'And the Philistines seized him and gouged out his eyes and brought him down to Gaza and bound him with bronze shackles. And he ground at the mill in the prison.' nāqar ʿênayim (H5365 H5869, to gouge out eyes) — blinding captured enemies was standard ANE practice to prevent escape and further resistance (cf. 2 Kgs 25:7, Zedekiah blinded). The irony is complete: Samson who saw and desired women (14:1; 16:1,4) loses the very faculty that drove him. ṭāḥan (H2912, to grind — mill work was done by women and slaves, Exod 11:5; Isa 47:2) — Samson the mighty judge is reduced to slave labor at a hand-mill, the most humiliating work possible.
16.23: wəsarnê pəlištîm neʾespû lizbaḥ zebaḥ-gāḏôl lədāgôn ʾĕlōhêhem ûləśimḥāh wayyōʾmərû nātan ʾĕlōhênû bəyāḏênû ʾet-šimšôn ʾôyəḇênû — 'The lords of the Philistines gathered to offer a great sacrifice to Dagon their god and to rejoice. They said: Our god has given Samson our enemy into our hand.' Dāgôn (H1712) — the principal deity of the Philistines, cognate with dāgān (H1715, grain/cereal) or possibly with Ugaritic dgn. nātan ʾĕlōhênû — 'our god has given' — the Philistines credit their deity for what YHWH accomplished through Samson's covenant violation. The theological irony is layered: it was Samson's abandonment of YHWH's covenant that enabled his capture; the Philistines read YHWH's withdrawal as Dagon's victory.
16.24: wayyirʾû ʾōtô hāʿām wayyəhalləlû ʾet-ʾĕlōhêhem kî ʾāmərû nātan ʾĕlōhênû bəyāḏênû ʾet-ʾôyəḇênû — 'When the people saw him, they praised their god. For they said: Our god has given our enemy into our hand.' The public celebration of Samson's capture is a national religious event for Philistia: the praise (hillēl H1984, to praise — from which hallēlûyāh) directed to Dagon is the Philistine counter-liturgy to Israel's praise of YHWH. The narrative sets up the dramatic reversal: the very celebration that mocks YHWH will become the occasion for YHWH's final vindication through Samson.
16.25: wayhî kîṭôḇ libbām wayyōʾmərû qirəʾû ləšimšôn wîśaḥeq-lānû wayyiqrəʾû ləšimšôn mibbêt hāʾăsûrîm wayyəṣaḥeq lipnêhem — 'When their hearts were merry, they said: Call Samson, that he may entertain us. So they called Samson out of the prison, and he entertained them. They made him stand between the pillars.' śāḥaq (H7832, to play/make sport — but also to mock/laugh at; cf. Gen 21:9 Ishmael 'playing' which got him expelled) — Samson is brought out as entertainment, a spectacle for Philistine amusement. The position between the pillars (bên hāʿammûḏîm) is the strategic position from which Samson will pull the temple down. What the Philistines arrange as public humiliation, YHWH arranges as the setup for the final act.
16.26: wayyōʾmer šimšôn ʾel-hannáʿar hammahăzîq bəyāḏô hanîḥāh-ʾôtî wəhāmišēnî ʾet-hāʿammûḏîm ʾăšer habbayyit nākôn ʿălêhem wəʾeššāʿēn ʿălêhem — 'Samson said to the young man who held him by the hand: Let me feel the pillars on which the house rests, that I may lean against them.' The blind Samson must be led by a young attendant — his dependence is total. māšaš (H4184, to feel/touch — the touch of a blind person groping) — the request to 'feel the pillars' is framed as needing support; his actual purpose is to find the structural load-bearing columns. The young attendant brings him to exactly the right place.
16.27: wəhayyit māle haʾanāšîm wəhannāšîm wəšām kol-sarnê pəlištîm wəʿal-hagagg kišəlōšet ʾălāpîm ʾîš wəʾiššāh hārōʾîm bəśeḥôq šimšôn — 'Now the house was full of men and women. All the lords of the Philistines were there, and on the roof there were about three thousand men and women, who looked on while Samson entertained.' The crowd count establishes the scale of the catastrophe: approximately 3,000 on the roof, plus the floor level — the total death toll in v30 ('those he killed at his death were more than those he killed in his life') is enabled by this massive gathering. All five Philistine lords are present (v27) — the decapitation of the Philistine leadership structure is total.
16.29: wayyilpōt šimšôn ʾet-šənê ʿammûḏê haṯṯāwek ʾăšer habbayyit nākôn ʿălêhem wayyissāʿēn ʿălêhem — 'Samson grasped the two middle pillars on which the house rested, and he leaned his weight against them, his right hand on the one and his left hand on the other.' ʿammûḏê haṯṯāwek (H5982 H8432, the middle pillars — the central load-bearing columns of the temple structure) — Philistine temples of this period are archaeologically attested with two central pillars supporting the roof (cf. the temple excavated at Tell Qasile). Samson embraces both pillars simultaneously — a final act requiring the arm-span strength that defined his whole career. wayyissāʿēn ʿălêhem (he leaned upon them) — the posture before the push.
16.31: wayyērəḏû ʾeḥāyw wəkol-bêt ʾāḇîw wayyiśśəʾû ʾōtô wayyaʿălû wayyiqbərû ʾôtô bên-ṣorʿāh ûḇên-ʾeštāʾōl bəqeḇer mānôaḥ ʾāḇîw wəhûʾ šāpaṭ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl ʿeśrîm šānāh — 'Then his brothers and all his father's house came down and took him and brought him up and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the tomb of Manoah his father. He had judged Israel twenty years.' Samson is buried in his ancestral ground, between Zorah and Eshtaol where the Spirit first stirred him (13:25). The family comes — his family, not Israel's tribes. No national mourning, no statement that 'the land had rest' — only the repeated twenty-year formula. Samson's epitaph is a judge who judged and died, personally significant but never nationally transformative. The Spirit that first stirred at Zorah-Eshtaol is the Spirit that ended at Gaza.

17.2: wayyōʾmer ʾel-ʾimmô ʾelep ûmēʾāh hakkāsep ʾăšer luqqaḥ-lāk wattəʾallî wəgam ʾāmart bəʾāznāy hinnēh hakkesef ʾittî ʾănî laqāḥtîw — 'He said to his mother: The 1100 pieces of silver that were taken from you, about which you uttered a curse in my hearing — behold, the silver is with me; I took it.' ʾālāh (H422, to curse/swear/bind with an oath) — the mother had invoked a curse on the thief; Micah confesses to avoid the curse's effect. kāsep (H3701, silver/money) — 1100 pieces of silver is exactly the amount each Philistine lord paid Delilah (16:5), creating a literary echo: the narrative moves from Samson's destruction by silver to Micah's corruption by silver.
17.3: wayyāšeb ʾet-ʾelep ûmēʾāh hakkāsep ləʾimmô wattoʾmer ʾimmô haqḏēš hiqdaštî ʾet-hakkesef laYHWH miyyāḏî liḇnî laʿăśôt pāsael ûmassēkāh — 'He returned the 1100 pieces of silver to his mother, and his mother said: I truly dedicate the silver to YHWH from my hand for my son, to make a carved image and a molten image.' hiqḏîš (H6942, to consecrate/set apart as holy — Hiphil) — dedicating money to YHWH, then immediately directing it toward idol manufacture, is the chapter's central theological contradiction. pāsal (H6459 root, carved/graven image) and massēkāh (H4541, cast/molten image — from nāsak H5258, to pour out) — both types of idol are prohibited by the second commandment (Exod 20:4; Deut 5:8). The mother uses YHWH's name to consecrate what YHWH forbids.
17.5: wəhāʾîš mîkāh lô bêt ʾĕlōhîm wayyaʿaś ʾêpôḏ ûtərāpîm wayyəmallēʾ ʾet-yaḏ ʾaḥaḏ mibānāyw wayyəhî-lô ləkōhēn — 'And the man Micah had a house of gods; he made an ephod and household gods, and he installed one of his sons who became his priest.' bêt ʾĕlōhîm (house of God/gods — the same phrase as Bethel, Gen 28:17) — a private shrine mimicking the tabernacle. ʾêpôḏ (H646, ephod — the priestly garment worn by the high priest, Exod 28:4-14) — in Judges 8:27, Gideon made an ephod that 'became a snare'; here Micah makes another. tərāpîm (H8655, household gods/idols — the same objects Rachel stole from Laban, Gen 31:19; used for divination in 1 Sam 15:23; Ezek 21:21) — keeping household gods was a form of domestic religious syncretism common in ancient Canaan. millēʾ yāḏ (H4390 H3027, to fill the hand — idiom for ordaining/installing a priest, Exod 28:41; 29:9) — Micah installs his son in a priestly role, violating the Levitical monopoly on priesthood (Num 3:5-10).
17.6: bayyāmîm hāhēm ʾên meleḵ bəyiśrāʾēl ʾîš hayyāšār bəʿênāyw yaʿăśeh — 'In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did what was right in his own eyes.' The refrain appears here for the first time in its full form (cf. 18:1; 19:1; 21:25). meleḵ (H4428, king) — the absence of kingship is the structural diagnosis: without a human king, there is no covenant enforcement, no Deuteronomic accountability. hayyāšār bəʿênāyw (what is right in his own eyes) — each person substitutes personal moral judgment for covenant law. This is a reversal of the Shema: Israel should do what is right in YHWH's eyes (Deut 12:8,25,28; 13:18) — the formula here substitutes ʿênāyw (his eyes) for ʿênê YHWH (the eyes of YHWH). The theological summary indicts the entire chs17-21 appendix as the consequence of lawless self-rule.
17.13: wayyōʾmer mîkāh ʿattāh yāḏaʿtî kî-yêṭîḇ YHWH lî kî hāyāh lî haLēwî ləkōhēn — 'Micah said: Now I know that YHWH will do me good, because I have a Levite as priest.' Micah's theological logic is entirely superstitious: having the right ritual personnel guarantees divine blessing, regardless of what those personnel are doing. yêṭîḇ YHWH lî (YHWH will do good for me) — a prosperity assumption disconnected from covenant obedience. The Levite being hired as a private priest is itself a violation (Levites were to serve at the tabernacle under the high priest's supervision, not in private shrines). Yet Micah is satisfied because he now has a 'real' priest for his idolatrous shrine — the tragedy of religious formalism that substitutes correct procedure for correct worship.
17.1: wayhî-ʾîš mêhar ʾeprayim ûšəmô mîkāyəhû — 'There was a man of the hill country of Ephraim whose name was Micayahu.' mîkāyəhû (H4318, Micayahu — 'who is like YHWH?') — the name is a rhetorical question praising YHWH's incomparability (cf. Exod 15:11: mî-kāmōkā bāʾēlim YHWH). The irony is thick: a man whose name asks 'who is like YHWH?' will fill his house with idols. The hill country of Ephraim was Israelite heartland territory, not a borderland — this corruption is not a peripheral problem but central to Israel's society.
17.4: wayyāšeb ʾet-hakkesef ləʾimmô wayyiqqaḥ ʾimmô mēʾātayim kāsep wattitnēhû laṣṣôrep wayyaʿaśēhû pāsal ûmassēkāh wayhî bəbêt mîkāyəhû — 'He returned the silver to his mother, and his mother took 200 pieces of silver and gave them to the silversmith, who made them into a carved image and a molten image. And it was in the house of Micah.' ṣôrep (H6884, silversmith/refiner — from ṣārap, to refine metal, test by fire) — the professional craftsman executes the commission. Only 200 of the 1100 pieces go toward the idol; the rest remains with the mother. The idols are installed in the domestic space: bêt mîkāyəhû (the house of Micah) — the household shrine begins with professional-grade idols, immediately setting up the escalation in v5.
17.7: wayhî-naʿar mibbêt leḥem yəhûḏāh mimmišpaḥat yəhûḏāh wəhûʾ lēwî wəhûʾ gār-šam — 'Now there was a young man from Bethlehem in Judah, from the clan of Judah. He was a Levite, and he was sojourning there.' lēwî (H3878, Levite) — the Levites had no tribal territorial allotment; they were to be distributed among Israel's cities with their own towns and pasture lands (Josh 21). A Levite from Bethlehem who is 'sojourning there' — in his own tribe's territory — suggests this Levite is displaced or rootless, not settled in his designated Levitical city. This instability makes him available for hire, setting up the private chaplaincy arrangement.
17.8: wayyēlek hāʾîš mēhāʿîr mibbêt leḥem yəhûḏāh lāgûr baʾăšer yimṣāʾ wayyāḇōʾ har-ʾeprayim ʿaḏ-bêt mîkāh laʿăśôt darkô — 'The man departed from the city of Bethlehem in Judah to sojourn wherever he could find a place. As he traveled, he came to the hill country of Ephraim to the house of Micah.' lāgûr baʾăšer yimṣāʾ — 'to sojourn wherever he could find' — the Levite is economically migrating: searching for a place of residence and provision. The Levitical system required Israel to support the Levites (Deut 14:29; 18:1-8), but in the period of 'no king in Israel,' that system has broken down. The Levite arrives at Micah's house by apparently random travel (darkô, 'his road/way') — but the narrative will show YHWH's judgment working through this encounter.
17.9: wayyōʾmer lô mîkāh mēʾayin tāḇôʾ wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw lēwî ʾānôkî mibbêt leḥem yəhûḏāh wəʾānōkî hōlēk lāgûr baʾăšer ʾimṣāʾ — 'Micah said to him: Where do you come from? He said to him: I am a Levite from Bethlehem in Judah, and I am going to sojourn wherever I may find a place.' The Levite's self-identification as a Levite (lēwî ʾānôkî) immediately interests Micah: he needs a legitimate priest for his shrine, and a genuine Levite is better than his son (v5). The exchange is transactional from the start: Micah asks 'where from?' — a standard hospitality question — and the Levite's answer triggers Micah's opportunistic offer in v10.
17.10: wayyōʾmer lô mîkāh šəḇāh ʿimmādî wahyēh lî ləʾāḇ ûləkōhēn wəʾānōkî ʾettēn-ləkā ʿăśeret kəsep layyāmîm ûʿerek bəḡāḏîm ûmiḥyātekā — 'Micah said to him: Stay with me, and be to me a father and a priest, and I will give you ten pieces of silver a year and a suit of clothes and your food.' ləʾāḇ ûləkōhēn (to be a father and a priest) — 'father' here is a term of honor and authority: a spiritual advisor and authority figure in the household. The employment contract is explicit: ten silver pieces annually, one set of clothing, and food — a standard service arrangement. The Levite is hired as a domestic chaplain for an idolatrous shrine: his priestly legitimacy is being rented to cover an illegitimate religious enterprise.
17.11: wayyôʾel halēwî lāšeḇet ʾet-hāʾîš wayhî hannáʿar lô kəʾaḥaḏ mibbānāyw — 'And the Levite agreed to stay with the man, and the young man was to him like one of his sons.' wayyôʾel (H2974, to be willing/consent/agree) — the Levite consents to the arrangement. kəʾaḥaḏ mibbānāyw (like one of his sons) — Micah adopts the young Levite into quasi-filial status: the previous family-priest son (v5) is replaced by a professional. The family relationship language masks a contractual arrangement: the Levite is an employee, not a family member, despite the domestic metaphor. This pseudo-adoption language will collapse when a better offer comes (ch18).
17.12: wayyəmallēʾ mîkāh ʾet-yaḏ halēwî wayhî-lô hannáʿar ləkōhēn wayhî bəbêt mîkāh — 'And Micah filled the hand of the Levite, and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah.' millēʾ yāḏ (to fill the hand — the ordination formula, Exod 28:41; 29:9; Lev 8:33) — Micah formally installs the Levite using proper ordination language for an entirely improper purpose. The ordination formula belongs to the tabernacle consecration of Aaron and his sons under divine command; Micah applies it to a private shrine with hired personnel. The gap between correct ritual language and corrupt religious practice is the chapter's defining irony.

11.1: gibbôr ḥayil (H1368 + H2428) — “mighty warrior/man of valor” — the same honorific used of Gideon (6:12) and Boaz (Ruth 2:1). The juxtaposition with bên-ʾiššāh zônāh (son of a prostitute, H802) is abrupt: the text establishes Jephthah’s military credential before naming his social liability. Gilʿādî (the Gileadite) at the end of the verse completes the triple identification — Jephthah is a Gileadite warrior of prowess whose birth excludes him from the inheritance he should have had.
11.2: naḥălāh (H5159, inheritance) governs the conflict: Gilead’s legitimate sons deny Jephthah his portion. The Hebrew phrase lōʾ-tinḥal bəḇêt-ʾāḇînû — “you will not inherit in our father’s house” — is a formal legal exclusion. Israelite inheritance law tied naḥălāh to the status of the mother; a son of a slave-woman or prostitute could be excluded if the father’s legitimate heirs objected (cf. Ishmael, Gen 21:10).
11.3: ʾănāšîm rêqîm (H376 + H7386, empty/worthless men) — the same phrase used for Abimelech’s retinue (9:4). The parallel is deliberate: like Abimelech, Jephthah attracts a marginal following when legitimacy is denied. Tôḇ as a place-name (location uncertain, perhaps in northern Transjordan) means “good” — an ironic settlement name for a displaced outcast. wayyēṣēʾ ʾittô — they raided with him — establishes his career as a mercenary warlord before his commission.
11.4: Narrative transition: miyyāmîm (H3117, after some time/days) — the formula marks an unspecified lapse before the Ammonite crisis. The elders’ need reverses the power dynamic established in v2; the rejected son becomes the only viable military leader.
11.5: The elders of Gilead make the journey to retrieve Jephthah — a reversal that the narrator frames as driven by necessity (wayyāqumû, they rose up). The verb lāqaḥ (H3947, to take/fetch) acknowledges they are bringing him back on his terms, not theirs.
11.6: qāṣîn (H7101, commander/ruler) — the elders’ initial offer. The term is military and functional rather than covenantal; it offers command authority without social rehabilitation. Jephthah’s counter-negotiation in vv.8-11 upgrades this to rōʾš (head/chief), securing a permanent civic position alongside the military role.
11.7: Jephthah’s direct challenge (lāmmāh, why?) rehearses the injustice: śənēʾtem (you hated me, H8130) and gāraštem (you expelled me, H1644). Both verbs are perfects — completed acts Jephthah refuses to overlook. His return is not unconditional; he negotiates from grievance rather than gratitude.
11.8: The elders’ counter-offer expands: now rōʾš (H7218, head/chief) over all the inhabitants of Gilead, not merely military commander. The political elevation is the concession Jephthah required; it transforms a military commission into a permanent civil authority.
11.9: Jephthah frames the arrangement as conditional on YHWH’s action: ʾim-YHWH yittên ʾet-bənê ʿammôn — the victory is divine gift, not Jephthah’s personal achievement. This theological frame will re-appear in the vow (v30) and in the argument to the Ammonite king (vv.21-23): YHWH is the real warrior and judge.
11.10: YHWH yihyeh šōmēaʿ bênênû — “YHWH shall be the one hearing between us” — covenant-witness language. šōmēaʿ (H8085, hearing/witnessing) frames YHWH as the enforcer of the agreement; violation becomes not merely breach of promise but sin against YHWH. The verbal covenant sealed at Mizpah (v11) is thus given divine sanction.
11.11: rōʾš wəqāṣîn — the dual title ratifies both civic and military authority. waydabbēr Yiptāḥ ʾet-kol-dəḇārāyw lipnê YHWH bammizpāh — “Jephthah spoke all his words before YHWH at Mizpah” — the covenant terms are stated in YHWH’s presence, constituting a formal oath before the divine witness invoked in v10.
11.12: Diplomatic protocol: Jephthah sends messengers (malʾāḵîm) rather than attacking immediately — unusual for judges, who typically respond with force. This extended diplomatic exchange (vv.12-28) showcases Jephthah as a legal reasoner who argues Israel’s case from historical precedent before resorting to war.
11.13: The Ammonite claim: Israel took their land when coming from Egypt — specifically the territory from the Arnon to the Jabbok and Heshbon. Historically, this was Amorite territory (Sihon’s kingdom) that Israel captured in Num 21, not Ammonite land; Jephthah’s response will press exactly this distinction.
11.14: Second embassy — the repetition of messengers (šûḇ, again) emphasizes Jephthah’s exhaustion of diplomatic channels before armed conflict. The legal argument in vv.15-27 is the fullest diplomatic speech in Judges.
11.15: Jephthah’s denial is categorical: lōʾ-lāqaḥ Yiśrāʾēl ʾet-ʾereṣ Môʾāḇ wəʾet ʾereṣ bənê ʿammôn — “Israel did not take the land of Moab or the land of the Ammonites.” The legal strategy is to distinguish between Moabite/Ammonite territory (which Israel bypassed per Num 20-21) and Amorite territory (which Israel conquered from Sihon).
11.16: The wilderness itinerary recapitulates Num 20-21: Israel went through the desert from Egypt to the Red Sea and then to Kadesh — respecting the borders of Edom and Moab throughout. This is the foundational claim of the legal brief: Israel’s route of travel did not encroach on Ammonite land.
11.17: The request to pass through (naʿbərāh-nāʾ bəʾarṣeḵā) was refused by the king of Edom; a parallel request to Moab was also refused — but neither Edom nor Moab went to war over Israel’s presence. Jephthah uses the precedent to distinguish refusal to yield passage (which Israel accepted) from active military contest (which only Sihon initiated).
11.18: The wilderness detour around Edom and Moab is mapped precisely: past Edom, past Moab, arriving on the eastern side of the Arnon — always outside the Ammonite border. The geographical specificity grounds the legal argument; Jephthah knows the itinerary texts of the Pentateuch.
11.19: The Sihon embassy (šəlaḥ Yiśrāʾēl malʾāḵîm ʾel-Sîḥôn, cf. Num 21:21-22) is identical to the earlier requests: a request for passage, not conquest. The distinction matters legally — Israel’s intent was transit, not territorial seizure; Sihon transformed the encounter into a war by refusing and attacking.
11.20: wəlōʾ-hehĕʾmîn Sîḥôn — “Sihon did not trust Israel to pass through his territory.” hehĕʾmîn (H539 Hiphil, to believe/trust) is the verbal root of ʾĕmet (faithfulness); Sihon’s failure of trust provoked the war. This reframes the conquest as defensive rather than aggressive on Israel’s part.
11.21: wayyittên YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ʾet-Sîḥôn wəʾet-kol-ʿammô — “YHWH the God of Israel gave Sihon and all his people into the hand of Israel.” The passive recipient of Amorite territory is YHWH’s gift to Israel, not Israel’s independent seizure — the legal and theological crux of the entire argument.
11.22: The territorial scope of the gift: from the Arnon to the Jabbok, from the wilderness to the Jordan. These are the same boundaries the Ammonite king claims in v13. Jephthah establishes that this was Amorite land (Sihon’s), not Ammonite, making the Ammonite territorial claim false on its own historical terms.
11.23: The theological summation: YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl hôrîš ʾet-hāʾĕmōrî — “YHWH the God of Israel dispossessed the Amorites.” hôrîš (H3423 Hiphil, to dispossess/drive out) places the action entirely with YHWH; the rhetorical question follows: “Are you to possess what YHWH our God has given us?”
11.24: The Chemosh argument: ʾăšer yôrîšeḵā Kəmôš ʾĕlōhêḵā — “whatever Chemosh your god gives you to possess.” This is a ius gentium (law of nations) argument, not a concession that Chemosh is real: Jephthah uses the Ammonite theology to make a parallel legal claim. The logic is “by your own principles, territorial possession follows divine grant.” This does not imply Israelite polytheism.
11.25: The Balak precedent (Bālāq ben-Ṣippôr) adds a historical-legal argument: even the most hostile Moabite king did not go to war to recover the territory Israel possessed for 300 years. Long non-contestation equals legal acquiescence in ancient near-eastern custom.
11.26: The 300-year possession claim (šəlōš meʾôt šānāh) asserts prescriptive title: Israel occupied Heshbon and the Transjordanian towns for three centuries without Ammonite challenge. The span corresponds roughly to the period from the conquest to Jephthah’s era. The rhetorical question “why did you not recover them in that time?” presses the laches argument.
11.27: YHWH haššōpēṭ — “YHWH the Judge” — Jephthah’s final appeal elevates the dispute to divine jurisdiction. šōpēṭ (H8199) is the same root as the term for Israel’s judges; YHWH is the supreme judge over inter-national disputes. Jephthah has exhausted legal argument; he submits the outcome to divine verdict.
11.28: wəlōʾ šāmaʿ melek bənê ʿammôn — “the king of the Ammonites did not listen.” The stark brevity after Jephthah’s elaborate legal argument marks the failure of diplomacy. šāmaʿ (H8085, to hear/heed) is the same verb used for YHWH’s attentiveness in v10; the Ammonite king’s deafness contrasts with YHWH’s role as the hearing witness.
11.29: rûaḥ YHWH hāyətāh ʿal-Yiptāḥ — “the Spirit of the LORD came upon Jephthah.” The identical construction appears at Othniel (3:10), Gideon (6:34), and Samson (13:25, 14:6, 19); the Spirit’s ḥāyāh ʿal (being upon someone) is the divine empowerment pattern for Israel’s deliverers. Jephthah’s subsequent military success is attributed to the Spirit, not his warrior skill alone.
11.30: wayyiddār Yiptāḥ neder laYHWH — “Jephthah vowed a vow to the LORD.” nādar neder (H5087 + H5088) is a cognate-object construction that intensifies the solemnity: not simply “he vowed” but “he vowed a vow” — comparable to “he dreamed a dream” or “he sinned a sin.” The vow is conditional on military victory: ʾim-nātôn tittēn (if you will indeed give) uses the infinitive absolute to signal certainty of intention.
11.31: The vow’s terms: hayyôṣēʾ ʾăšer yēṣēʾ middalṯê ḇêtî — “whatever goes out from the doors of my house.” The Hebrew has two consecutive clauses: wəhāyāh laYHWH (“it shall be the LORD’s”) and weheʿĕlîtîhû ʿōlāh (“and I will offer it as a burnt offering”). Whether the second waw is conjunctive (and) or disjunctive (or) determines whether Jephthah envisions one action (consecration-then-sacrifice) or two alternatives (if an animal, burnt offering; if a person, dedicated to YHWH). The text preserves the ambiguity without resolution.
11.32: wayyittēn YHWH ʾôtām bəyādô — “YHWH gave them into his hand.” The victory formula identical to the judge-cycle pattern; YHWH’s agency is foregrounded over Jephthah’s military action. The brief narrative of battle (v32-33) stands in contrast to the elaborate diplomatic speech; the actual war is almost incidental.
11.33: The victory geography: from Aroer to Minnith (20 cities) and Abel-keramim — a broad sweep across Transjordanian territory. makkāh gədôlāh məʾōd (a very great blow) is the deuteronomistic superlative indicating complete defeat. The Ammonite claim of v13 is answered by military outcome rather than legal verdict.
11.34: bittô yəḥîdāh lô — “she was his only child (yāḥîd).” yāḥîd (H3173, only/unique) is the same word used for Isaac in Gen 22:2 (your only son, whom you love). The echo is likely deliberate: the father who offered his only child, the child who accepted the sacrifice. tuppîm ûmāḥôlôt (tambourines and dancing) mark the homecoming celebration; the daughter comes to greet the returning warrior, the very scenario the vow implied.
11.35: wayyiqraʿ bigādāyw — “he tore his clothes” (mourning gesture). ʿāḵôr ʿăḵartinî — “you have brought me very low” — lit. “you have troubled/bent me.” pāṣîtî pî ʾel-YHWH — “I have opened my mouth to the LORD” — acknowledges the binding force of the vow; lōʾ ʾûḵal lāšûḇ — “I cannot take it back.” Jephthah treats the vow as irrevocable, which accords with Num 30:2 (a man must not break his word).
11.36: The daughter’s response demonstrates extraordinary covenantal integrity: ʿăśēh-lî kāʾăšer yāṣāʾ mippiḵā — “do to me according to what has gone out of your mouth.” She grounds her acceptance in the premise that YHWH has avenged Jephthah against his enemies; the vow and its fulfillment are inseparable from YHWH’s role as the one who gave the victory. Her language echoes the legal framework of the entire chapter.
11.37: The daughter’s request: two months to wander with her companions on the mountains and weep over her bəṯûlîm (H1331, virginity). The word choice is significant: she mourns her virginity/youth, not her impending death. If Jephthah intended human sacrifice, weeping over barrenness is an unusual focus; the text’s emphasis on virginity fits the consecration-to-lifelong-celibacy interpretation.
11.38: Permission granted; she goes for two months with her companions. wattēleḵ hîʾ wərêʿôtêhā watteḇke ʿal-bəṯûlêhā — the mourning is communal (with her companions) and specifically for her virginity, not for loss of life. The narrator’s precision here signals what is grieved.
11.39: wayyaʿaś lāh ʾet-nidərô ʾăšer nāḏār — “he did to her his vow which he had vowed.” The outcome statement is followed immediately by the clause wəhîʾ lōʾ yādəʿāh ʾîš — “and she had not known a man.” This parenthetical notation is the text’s interpretive key: the narrator marks her permanent virginity as the defining outcome, not her death. Had she been killed, the virginity clause would be irrelevant to the narrative. The execution of the vow is described with ʿāśāh (he did, H6213), which for a burnt offering would normally use ʿālāh or šāḥaṭ.
11.40: ḥōq bəYiśrāʾēl (H2706, a statute/custom in Israel) — tānāh (H8567, to commemorate/rehearse/lament) is an unusual verb; the daughters of Israel kept a four-day annual mourning. The word tānāh appears only here in the Hebrew Bible, possibly meaning “to rehearse in memory.” The custom memorializes specifically Jephthah’s daughter, not Jephthah — a mark of the narrator’s sympathy for the victim of the vow.

12.1: Ephraim’s complaint repeats the pattern of 8:1-3 (their grievance against Gideon): inter-tribal jealousy over military honor. ṣāpônāh (to the north) indicates they crossed northward toward Gilead. The threat to burn Jephthah’s house escalates the confrontation from 8:1-3, where Gideon pacified Ephraim with diplomacy; Jephthah will not.
12.2: Jephthah’s rebuttal is terse and factual: ʾîš rîḇ hāyîtî waʿammî ûḇənê ʿammôn məʾōd — “I and my people were in great conflict with the Ammonites.” His claim that he called Ephraim and they did not come is unverified by the narrative of ch11 — but the narrator neither confirms nor denies it, leaving Jephthah’s assertion uncontested.
12.3: wāʾāśîmāh napšî bəḵappî — “I put my life in my own hands.” This idiom (lit. “placed my soul in my palm”) expresses risking one’s life; nāpāš (H5315) as the seat of life, kap (H3709) as the open palm. Compare 1 Sam 19:5 (Jonathan uses the same idiom for David). The phrase underlines that Jephthah acted without Ephraimite support and won anyway.
12.4: ʾattā pəlîṭê ʾeprayim ʾattā giləʿād — “You are fugitives of Ephraim, O Gilead.” This tribal insult — that Gileadites were Ephraimite refugees — was the provocation for the battle. The Transjordanian Israelites were viewed by Cisjordanian tribes as marginal; Ephraim’s slur triggers a civil war. wayyakke giləʿād ʾet-ʾeprayim — “Gilead struck Ephraim” — civil war, Israel killing Israel.
12.5: The Gileadites secured the Jordan fords (maʿabrôt hayyardēn) — strategic chokepoints across which Ephraimite survivors had to pass. The fords are the same terrain used in Gideon’s pursuit of Midian (7:24-25); controlling them means controlling who escapes.
12.6: ʾĕmār-nāʾ šibbōlet — “Say now Shibboleth.” šibbōlet (H7641) means either “ear of grain” or “flowing stream” — the phonological content mattered less than the dialectal distinction. Ephraimite dialect lacked the /ʃ/ phoneme, producing sibbōlet instead; the Gileadite ear detected the substitution. The result: 42,000 Ephraimite dead at the fords. This is the Hebrew Bible’s most explicit account of a dialectal accent being weaponized.
12.7: Jephthah’s tenure: six years. The formulaic close (wayyišpōṭ + years + death + burial) reduces one of Judges’ most complex figures to a brief summary. Burial “in one of the cities of Gilead” — the vagueness contrasts with the precision of minor-judge burial notices in vv.8-15, perhaps a textual lacuna.
12.8: Ibzan of Bethlehem — probably Bethlehem in Zebulun (Josh 19:15) rather than the Judean Bethlehem, given the Zebulunite context. šāpaṭ (H8199, judged) is the only term applied; no Spirit, no military action, no divine evaluation.
12.9: Thirty sons and thirty daughters: the large family is a status indicator in the ancient world, evidence of prosperity and influence. The exogamous marriage pattern — daughters sent out, daughters brought in from outside the clan — reflects political alliance-building through kinship ties. Ibzan commanded the resources to arrange sixty such alliances.
12.10: Burial at Bethlehem — the formulaic close for the minor judge cycle. The brevity of vv.8-10 mirrors the brevity of Ibzan’s recorded activity; these are administrative rather than narrative entries.
12.11: Elon the Zebulunite — ten-year tenure. No military action, no divine speech, no narrative. The sequence of minor judges (Ibzan, Elon, Abdon) creates a pattern of social stability between the turbulent stories of major judges; their formulaic notices mark passages of time in which Israel neither revolted nor was oppressed.
12.12: Buried at Aijalon in Zebulun — distinguished from Aijalon in Dan (Josh 19:42). The tribal identification haZzəḇûlônî frames the territory of his authority; burial location anchors the judge to his clan territory.
12.13: Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite — Pirathon is in the hill country of Ephraim (v15). Abdon’s Ephraimite origin immediately follows the Ephraimite civil war narrative of 12:1-7, creating an ironic reversal: the tribe that was nearly annihilated now supplies a judge.
12.14: Forty sons and thirty grandsons riding on seventy donkeys — the highest-status marker among the minor judges (Jair had thirty sons on thirty donkeys, 10:4; Abdon has seventy mounts across two generations). Riding a donkey signals aristocratic status in the ancient Near East (Num 22, 1 Sam 25:20); the number of mounts signals the breadth of his household’s honor.
12.15: Buried at Pirathon in Ephraim, in the Amalekite hill country — bəhar hāʿămālêqî notes that this region was once occupied by Amalek, a reminder that the land Israel inhabits came at a cost. The minor judge series closes here; Samson’s annunciation follows immediately as the next era begins.

13.1: The sin-cycle formula: wayyôsipû bənê Yiśrāʾēl laʿăśôt hāraʿ bəʿênê YHWH — “the Israelites again did evil in the sight of the LORD.” Forty years of Philistine oppression is the longest in Judges (cf. Midian 7 years, Ammon 18 years). The Philistines — sea-peoples settled on the Coastal Plain — represent a qualitatively different and more persistent threat than the Transjordanian enemies of earlier chapters.
13.2: ʿăqārāh (H6135, barren/sterile) — Manoah’s wife joins the barren-matriarch sequence: Sarah (Gen 11:30), Rebekah (Gen 25:21), Rachel (Gen 29:31), Hannah (1 Sam 1:2), Elizabeth (Luke 1:7). Each barrenness is a narrative marker for divine intervention; the child born from barrenness carries a special commission. wəhîʾ lōʾ yālādāh (she had borne no children) confirms the condition, not merely a state of delay.
13.3: malʾak YHWH (H4397 + H3068, Angel of the LORD) — the definite article identifies this as the recurring divine messenger of the Exodus and pre-Sinai narratives (Gen 16, 22; Exod 3; Judg 2, 6). The angel’s appearance to the woman first — before Manoah receives any word — is unusual; she receives the annunciation directly. wəhinnāḵ hārāh (and you shall conceive) uses the participial construction of immediate future, identical to Isa 7:14 LXX.
13.4: The dietary prohibitions are addressed to the mother as preparation: haššāmərî nāʾ wəʾal-tištî yayin wəšēḵār — “be careful, drink no wine or strong drink.” šēḵār (H7941, strong drink) is any fermented beverage other than wine. The Nazirite regulations will bind the child from the womb; the mother’s observance during pregnancy extends the Nazirite restrictions to gestation.
13.5: nāzîr ʾĕlōhîm (H5139, consecrated one of God) — Nazirite, from nāzar (to separate, consecrate). Unlike the voluntary Nazirite vow of Num 6 (which is temporary), Samson’s consecration is lifelong and prenatal — imposed before birth. hûʾ yāḥēl ləhôšîaʿ ʾet-Yiśrāʾēl — “he will begin to save Israel.” yāḥēl (H2490, begin) signals incomplete deliverance; Samson’s salvation is inaugural, not final. hôšîaʿ (H3467) is the verbal root of Joshua (yəhôšûaʿ) and Jesus (Iēsous).
13.6: The woman’s report to Manoah: ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm (man of God, H376 + H430) — the conventional prophetic title; she does not yet recognize him as an angel. mōrāʾ məʾōd (very awesome/terrifying) — she perceives the visitor’s superhuman nature without identifying it. wəlōʾ šəʾaltîhû ʾayyeh-miZZeh — “I did not ask him where he was from” — the convention in theophany encounters is that the divine visitor’s origin is not to be interrogated.
13.7: The woman rehearses the message: the angel’s prohibition on wine and unclean food, the Nazirite status from birth. The repetition in vv.4-5 and 7 is a narrative device that emphasizes the requirements and confirms the woman’s faithful transmission to her husband.
13.8: bî ʾădōnāy — “O Lord.” Manoah’s prayer addresses YHWH as ʾădōnāy (H136, the Lord) — the reverent substitute for the divine name. His request is pastoral rather than skeptical: yəlammēdēnû mah-naʿăśeh lannnaʿar hayyûllād — “teach us what to do for the child who will be born.” He wants instruction for raising the commissioned child, not proof of the message.
13.9: wayyišmaʿ hāʾĕlōhîm bəqôl Mānôaḥ — “God listened to the voice of Manoah.” The Hiphil šāmaʿ here is divine attentiveness: prayer was heard and acted on. The angel returns, but again ʾel-hāʾiššāh (to the woman) — while she sits in the field, without Manoah present. The woman remains the primary recipient of the divine communication.
13.10: The woman acts with urgency (wattəmaher, she hurried) to bring Manoah. Her role is mediating — she receives and transmits the divine encounter to her husband. The narrative structure consistently gives her perceptive priority over Manoah, who will need guidance where she understood intuitively.
13.11: Manoah’s question: hattāh hāʾîš ʾăšer-dibbartā ʾel-hāʾiššāh — “Are you the man who spoke to this woman?” He addresses the angel as a man — not wrong at this point, since the angel appears in human form. The angel’s reply (ʾănî, I am) is laconic; he does not volunteer his identity.
13.12: mišpaṭ hannaʿar — “the rule for the child.” mišpaṭ (H4941, judgment/ordinance/custom) frames child-rearing as covenantally ordered; Manoah asks for the divinely appointed way of life for the consecrated child. This is a more theologically sophisticated question than simple curiosity.
13.13: The angel refers Manoah back to what the woman was already told — confirming the authority of her reception of the message. The doubling of instruction (vv.4-5 to the woman; v13 back to the woman) structurally underscores that she is the primary recipient.
13.14: The Nazirite requirements restated comprehensively: nothing from the vine, no wine or strong drink, nothing unclean — all three Num 6 food restrictions applied to the mother during pregnancy. The totality of the prenatal Nazirite framework is emphasized; nothing of the sacred separation is to be compromised.
13.15: Manoah’s hospitality: nəʿaṣṣərāh nāʾ ʾôtāḵā — “let us detain you.” The verb ʿāṣar (H6113, to detain/hold back) is the same used when Abraham urged the three visitors to stay (Gen 18:3-5). The offer of a kid for the meal recalls Abraham’s hospitality; Manoah does not yet know he is hosting the divine.
13.16: The angel refuses the meal but redirects: ʾim-taʿăśeh ʿôlāh laYHWH taʿălennāh — “if you offer a burnt offering, offer it to YHWH.” This refusal distinguishes the angel from a human guest who would eat, and from a pagan deity who would require a ritual meal; the burnt offering to YHWH is the appropriate form of worship. kî lōʾ yādaʿ Mānôaḥ — the narrator explains: Manoah still does not know he is speaking to the Angel of the LORD.
13.17: Manoah asks the angel’s name to know whom to honor when the promise is fulfilled: ləḵabbdəḵā — to honor you. The motive is appropriate (honoring one’s benefactor) but the angel’s response reveals that a name would be inadequate to contain his identity.
13.18: lāmmāh zeh tišʾal lišmî wəhûʾ-pelîʾî — “Why do you ask my name, seeing it is wonderful/beyond understanding?” pālîʾ / pelîʾî (H6383) derives from pālāʾ (H6381, to be wonderful, to be beyond ordinary comprehension). The same root appears in the messianic title of Isa 9:6: peleʾ yôʿēṣ (Wonderful Counselor). The angel’s name-refusal is itself a theophanic disclosure: his name is beyond human categories of understanding.
13.19: Manoah takes the kid and grain offering and offers them on the rock to YHWH — the rock as improvised altar recalls Gideon’s theophany (6:20-21). hammalpîʾ laʿăśôt — “the one who works wonders” — the participial phrase identifies YHWH/the angel as the subject of miraculous action during the sacrifice. wayyapleʾ (and he worked wondrously) confirms the theophany.
13.20: The fire theophany: baaʿălôt hallahav mēʿal hammizbaḥ — “as the flame went up from the altar.” The angel of the LORD ascended in the flame — echoing Elijah’s fire-descent from heaven (1 Kgs 18) and the pillar of fire in the Exodus. Manoah and his wife fell on their faces; prostration is the appropriate response to divine presence.
13.21: wəlōʾ-yāsap malʾak YHWH lĕhērāʾôt — “the Angel of the LORD did not appear again.” The cessation of appearances functions as interpretive closure: the angel’s work is complete. ʾāz yādaʿ Mānôaḥ kî-malʾak YHWH hûʾ — “then Manoah realized that he had been the Angel of the LORD.” The recognition arrives after the encounter ends, not during it — a recurring feature of theophany narratives (Gen 32:30; Luke 24:31).
13.22: mût nāmût — emphatic (absolute infinitive + finite): “we will certainly die.” Manoah’s fear is theologically grounded: the tradition that seeing God brings death (Exod 33:20; Judg 6:22). The phrase ʾĕlōhîm rāʾînû — “we have seen God” — confirms that the Angel of the LORD’s identity equates with seeing YHWH himself; the narrative does not distinguish the angel from God.
13.23: The wife’s response is a model of theological reasoning under fear: three parallel conditionals — if YHWH had wanted to kill us, he would not have (1) accepted the burnt offering, (2) shown us these things, (3) announced the coming child. Her logic moves from divine action to divine intention; what YHWH has already done is evidence of what he intends. The narrator gives the wife the final word in the theological debate between the two, a pattern of her superior perception throughout ch13.
13.24: watteqrāʾ ʾet-šəmô Šimšôn — “she named him Samson.” It is the mother who names the child — she received the annunciation, and she claims the naming. Šimšôn may derive from šemeš (H8121, sun), appropriate for a Danite hero from the Sorek valley near Bethshemesh (house of the sun). wayyiḡdal hannaʿar wayyəḇāreḵēhû YHWH — the divine blessing at birth is the same formula used for Samson as for Samuel (1 Sam 3:19) and John the Baptist (Luke 1:80).
13.25: wattāḥel rûaḥ YHWH lipʿămô — “the Spirit of the LORD began to stir him.” pāʿam (H6470, to beat/push/stir) describes the intermittent, recurrent movement of the Spirit; it is not a continuous indwelling but a periodic stirring — different from the Spirit coming upon a judge for a single event. bəmaḥănēh-dān (in the camp of Dan) between Zorah and Eshtaol — the encampment where the Spirit begins to move becomes the geographic theater for Samson’s coming exploits.

18.1: bayyāmîm hāhēm ʾên meleḵ bəyiśrāʾēl ûḇayyāmîm hāhēm šēḇeṭ haddānî məḇaqquēš-lô nāḥălāh lāšeḇet kî lōʾ-nāpəlāh lô ʿaḏ hayyôm hahûʾ bəṯôk šibṭê yiśrāʾēl bənāḥălāh — 'In those days there was no king in Israel, and in those days the tribe of Dan was seeking for itself an inheritance (nāḥălāh) to live in, for to that day no inheritance had fallen to them among the tribes of Israel.' The chapter opens with the double refrain (ʾên meleḵ bəyiśrāʾēl) and immediately maps its consequence: without covenant order, even territorial inheritance breaks down. nāḥălāh (H5159, inheritance — the covenantal land-gift, what YHWH promised and Joshua distributed) — Dan's failure to secure their allotment (Josh 19:40-48; cf. Judg 1:34 — the Amorites pushed them back) means they exist as a landless tribe, contrary to covenant purpose. Their solution (migration north) is presented without divine sanction.
18.2: wayyišləḥû bənê-ḏān mimmišpaḥtām ḥămiššāh ʾănāšîm miqṣôtām ʾanšê bənê-ḥayil — 'The sons of Dan sent from their clan five men from their extremities, men of valor.' miqqəṣôtām (from their extremities/fringes — the whole tribe represented by men from the outer edges) — the extremity language suggests comprehensive representation. ʾanšê bənê-ḥayil (H2428 H1121, men of valor/capability) — the same phrase used of Boaz (Ruth 2:1) and Gideon's greeting (Judg 6:12). The reconnaissance mission echoes the Exodus-era spies (Num 13), but without Moses's authorization or YHWH's command — a structural parallel that underscores the absence of divine guidance.
18.6: wayyōʾmer lāhem hakkōhēn ləkû ləšālôm nekōaḥ YHWH darkəkem ʾăšer tēlkû-ḇāh — 'The priest said to them: Go in peace. The way in which you go is before YHWH.' The Levite-priest pronounces an oracle of favorable outcome — but the text provides no indication that he actually inquired of YHWH, nor is the ʾêpôḏ or tərāpîm (v5 — 'they inquired of God' via Micah's unauthorized shrine) a legitimate instrument of divine revelation. nekōaḥ YHWH (before/in the sight of YHWH) is the priestly blessing formula used to validate the journey — but the entire apparatus (private shrine, private Levite, idols) is corrupt. The oracle may be technically fulfilled, yet its source is polluted.
18.7: wayyēlkû ḥămiššāh hāʾănāšîm wayyāḇōʾû lāyišāh wayyirʾû ʾet-hāʿām ʾăšer-bəqirḇāh yōšeḇet lāḇeṭaḥ — 'The five men went and came to Laish and saw the people who were in it, dwelling in security (yōšeḇet lāḇeṭaḥ).' lāḇeṭaḥ (H983, in security/trust — the state of dwelling without fear of attack) — this word is used of Israel dwelling securely under YHWH's protection (Deut 33:12,28; Judg 8:11). The people of Laish are described with four vulnerability markers: dwelling securely, manner of Sidon (peaceful/commercial not military), quiet and unsuspecting (šōqēṭ wəḇōṭēaḥ), no lack of anything, far from Sidon and from anyone who might rescue them (miyyad mêṣar — no deliverer). This accumulation of vulnerability terms prepares the reader to be troubled, not cheered, by what follows.
18.14: wayaʿănû ḥămiššāh hāʾănāšîm hahōlkîm lərāggēl ʾet-ʾereṣ lāyiš wayyōʾmərû ʾel-ʾăḥêhem — 'Then the five men who went to spy out the land of Laish answered and said to their brothers.' The men know Micah's house contains a pesel ûmassēkāh ʾêpôḏ ûtərāpîm (graven image, molten image, ephod, and teraphim) and direct the tribe to take them. The casual recommendation to steal a shrine's sacred objects — instruments of covenant violation — is presented as pragmatic military intelligence, not sacrilege. That 600 armed men will stop to plunder a private household's religious objects on their way to conquer a city reveals the total inversion of values that marks the book's closing chapters.
18.19: wayyōʾmərû lô ḥăraš śîm-yāḏəkā ʿal-pîkā ûlēk ʿimmānû wihyēh-lānû ləʾāḇ ûləkōhēn — 'They said to him: Be silent; put your hand on your mouth, and come with us and be to us a father and a priest.' ḥărēš (H2790, be silent) — the command to silence is coercive: be quiet about this theft. ləʾāḇ ûləkōhēn (father and priest) — the Levite will gain a tribe-level priestly role rather than a household one. His ready acceptance (wayyîṭaḇ lēḇ hakkōhēn — the priest's heart was glad, v20) reveals his mercenary character: priesthood is portable and negotiable, not a covenant vocation. The offer parodies legitimate Levitical ministry: Aaron was 'mouth and priest' to Moses; this Levite becomes 'priest' to stolen idols.
18.27: wəhēmmāh lāqəḥû ʾēt ʾăšer-ʿāśāh mîkāh wəʾet-hakkōhēn ʾăšer hāyāh-lô wayyāḇōʾû ʿal-lāyiš ʿal-ʿam šōqēṭ ûḇōṭēaḥ wayyakkû ʾôtām ləpî-ḥāreḇ — 'They took what Micah had made, and the priest who had been his, and came to Laish, to a people quiet and unsuspecting, and struck them with the edge of the sword.' ḥāreḇ (H2719, sword) — the conquest of Laish with the sword echoes the Canaanite conquests of Joshua, but without YHWH's command, without legitimate territory, against a peaceful people. ʾên maṣṣîl (H5337, no one delivering/rescuing) — Laish had no deliverer, as the spies noted (v28). The sad irony: Israel, who was delivered from Egypt by YHWH when no human could save them, now acts as the unchecked oppressor of an undefended people.
18.30: wayyāqîmû lāhem bənê-ḏān ʾet-happāsel wayyōnāṯān ben-gərōšōm ben-mōšeh hûʾ ûḇānāyw hāyû kōhănîm laššēḇeṭ haddānî — 'The sons of Dan set up the carved image for themselves, and Jonathan son of Gershom, son of Moses, he and his sons were priests to the tribe of Dan.' The genealogical bombshell: the anonymous 'Levite' of chs17-18 is revealed as Jonathan son of Gershom son of Moses — Moses's own grandson is the first idolatrous priest of Danite Israel. Some manuscripts read mənašše (Manasseh) with a superscripted nun — a scribal protective alteration to spare Moses the shame. But the earliest tradition (and the Qere) reads mōšeh: Moses's grandson. The priestly dynasty of Micah's idolatrous shrine serves Dan ʿaḏ yôm gəlôt hāʾāreṣ — until the day of the land's exile (Assyrian conquest, 722 BCE).
18.31: wayyāśîmû lāhem ʾet-pesel mîkāh ʾăšer ʿāśāh kol-yəmê heyôt bêt-hāʾĕlōhîm bəšilōh — 'They set up Micah's carved image that he made, all the days that the house of God was at Shiloh.' The conclusion is devastating: while the legitimate tabernacle stood at Shiloh (bêt-hāʾĕlōhîm bəšilōh), Dan maintained its idolatrous shrine in the north. Two cult sites, one legitimate and authorized, one stolen and unauthorized — the parallel existence of covenant and apostasy across the tribal period. Shiloh's eventual destruction (1 Sam 4; Jer 7:12-14) does not close the northern shrine; the Danite idolatry outlasts the legitimate sanctuary, a testimony to how deeply apostasy takes root.

19.1: wayhî bayyāmîm hāhēm ûmeleḵ ʾên bəyiśrāʾēl wayhî ʾîš lēwî gār bəyarktê har-ʾeprayim wayyiqqaḥ-lô ʾiššāh pîlegeš mibêt leḥem yəhûḏāh — 'It came to pass in those days when there was no king in Israel that a certain Levite was sojourning (gār) in the remote parts of the hill country of Ephraim, and he took a concubine (pîlegeš) from Bethlehem in Judah.' The no-king formula sets the moral context. pîlegeš (H6370, concubine — a secondary wife with sexual rights but no inheritance claims; the word may be related to Akkadian pilakku, spindle-woman) — her status is ambiguous legally: she has the intimacy of a wife without full covenant protection. gār (H1481, to sojourn — to live in a place without full citizenship) — the Levite is himself a sojourner, dependent on hospitality, without territorial roots; his social vulnerability mirrors hers.
19.2: wattizneh ʿālāyw pîlagšô wattellex mēʾittô ʾel-bêt ʾāḇîhā ʾel-bêt leḥem yəhûḏāh — 'His concubine played the harlot against him (wattizneh) and went from him to her father's house in Bethlehem in Judah.' zānāh (H2181, to play the harlot/be unfaithful) — the verb can mean literal prostitution, infidelity in marriage, or in a softer reading simply 'became angry with him and left.' The LXX reads 'she was angry with him' — some scholars prefer this softer sense given his later tender retrieval of her (v3: 'to speak to her heart,' a covenant love idiom). The four-month separation (yāmîm ʾarbaʿāh ḥodāšîm) implies an extended estrangement, not a one-night departure.
19.3: wayyāqom ʾîšāh wayyelek ʾaḥărêhā lədabbēr ʿal-libbāh — 'Her husband arose and went after her to speak to her heart.' ḏibbēr ʿal-lēḇ (H1696 H3820, to speak to/upon the heart) is the OT idiom for words of tender comfort and persuasion — used of Isaac comforting Rebekah (Gen 24:67, LXX), of Shechem wooing Dinah (Gen 34:3), of Boaz's kindness to Ruth (Ruth 2:13). The Levite goes to win back his alienated wife with words of covenant love. He brings a servant and two donkeys (ḥămôr) — the travel equipment of a man serious about the journey. The father-in-law's repeated hospitality that delays their departure sets up the tragedy that follows.
19.14: wayyaʿaḇrû wayyēlkû ûtāḇōʾ lāhem haššemeš ʾeṣel haggibəʿāh ʾăšer ləḇinyāmîn — 'They passed on and went, and the sun set on them near Gibeah, which belongs to Benjamin.' The geographical detail is crucial: they chose Gibeah of Benjamin (giḇʿāh ʾăšer ləḇinyāmîn) over Jebus (Jerusalem, still a non-Israelite city). The servant had suggested Jebus; the Levite refused to enter a foreign city, choosing Gibeah — Israelite, Benjaminite, presumably safe (v12 — 'we will not turn aside into a city of foreigners who are not of the sons of Israel'). The choice of the 'safer' Israelite city leads to the worse outcome: the horror at Gibeah will surpass anything a Canaanite city could have done.
19.15: wayyāsurrû šām lāḇôʾ lālûn bəgiḇʿāh wayyāḇōʾ wayyēšeḇ birḥôḇ hāʿîr wəʾên ʾîš mēʾasēp ʾôtām habbayətāh lālûn — 'They turned aside there to go in to spend the night at Gibeah. He went in and sat in the town square, but there was no one who took them into his house to spend the night.' rəḥôḇ (H7339, the open square/broad place at a city gate) — sitting in the city square was the standard way to signal that you needed hospitality. wəʾên ʾîš mēʾasēp (no man gathering them in) — the failure of hospitality is the first sign of Gibeah's moral collapse. In the ancient Near East, hospitality to travelers was a near-absolute obligation; its absence signals a community that has ceased to function by covenant norms.
19.20: wayyōʾmer hazzāqēn šālôm lāk raq kol-maḥsôrkā ʿālay raq bāreḥôḇ ʾal-tālan — 'The old man said: Peace be to you; I will take care of all your needs; only do not spend the night in the square.' The old man's warning — 'do not spend the night in the square' — reveals that he knows the danger. He is a sojourner too (gēr v16 — from Ephraim, not a native Benjaminite), which may be why he, unlike the locals, shows hospitality: he knows what it is to need it. His injunction encodes the same vulnerability assessment the spies gave about Laish (18:7 — dwelling secure, no deliverer nearby) but in an Israelite city.
19.22: hēmmāh mêṭîḇîm ʾet-libbām wəhinnēh ʾanšê hāʿîr ʾanšê bənê-ḇəliyyaʿal nāsabû ʾet-habbayit mitdappəqîm ʿal-haddālet — 'While they were making their hearts merry, the men of the city, men who were sons of Belial (bənê bəliyyaʿal), surrounded the house, beating on the door.' bənê bəliyyaʿal (H1121 H1100, sons of worthlessness/destruction — the term for those who have broken all moral constraints) is the same phrase used in Deut 13:13 for those who lead a city into idolatry and in 1 Sam 2:12 for Eli's sons. nāsab (H5437, to surround/encircle) + mitdappəqîm ʿal-haddālet (beating on the door) — the scene directly parallels Gen 19:4-9 where Sodom's men surrounded Lot's house. The echo is intentional: Gibeah of Benjamin has become Sodom. An Israelite city in the Promised Land reproduces the worst act of Canaan's condemned cities.
19.24: hinnēh bittî habbəṯûlāh wəpilagšēhû ʾôṣîʾāh nāʾ ʾôtām wəʿannû ʾôtām waaʿăśû lāhem haṭṭôḇ bəʿênêkem — 'Behold, here are my virgin daughter and his concubine. Let me bring them out. Violate them and do with them what is good in your eyes.' The host offers his daughter and the concubine to protect the male guest — an act that reflects the extreme ancient Near Eastern hospitality code (protecting the guest at all costs) but violates the equally fundamental code of protection of women. ʿinnāh (H6031, to afflict/violate/humble — used for sexual violation in Gen 34:2 Dinah; 2 Sam 13:12 Tamar) — the offer to 'violate' them with this word makes the proposed act explicit. haṭṭôḇ bəʿênêkem (what is good in your eyes) — echoes Judges' refrain: the crowd will do what is right in their own eyes, the ultimate collapse of covenant standards.
19.25: wəlōʾ ʾāḇû hāʾănāšîm lišmōaʿ lô wayyaḥăzēq hāʾîš bəpilagšô wayyōṣēʾ ʾălêhem haxûṣāh wayyēḏəʿû ʾôtāh wayyitʿallălû-ḇāh kol-hallaylāh ʿaḏ-habbōqer — 'But the men would not listen to him. So the man seized his concubine and thrust her out to them outside, and they knew her and abused her all night until the morning.' wayyaḥăzēq (H2388, he seized/grabbed — the verb of force) — the Levite's action is brutal: he seizes (ḥāzaq) his concubine, the one he came to retrieve with tender words, and thrusts her to the mob. wayyēḏəʿû ʾôtāh (H3045, they knew her — the same sexual euphemism as Gen 4:1 and Gen 19:5) — gang rape continuing all night. wayyitʿallălû-ḇāh (H5953, they abused/treated wickedly — from ʿālal, to act severely against) — the compound horror: the night-long assault destroys her.
19.28: wayyōʾmer ʾēlêhā qûmî wənēlēkāh wəʾên ʿōneh wayyiqqāḥêhā ʿal-haḥămôr wayyāqom hāʾîš wayyelek limqōmô — 'He said to her: Arise, let us be going. But there was no answer.' The brutal simplicity of the Levite's response to finding her at the threshold: 'Get up, let's go.' No grief expressed, no lamentation — then the most chilling detail in the chapter: wəʾên ʿōneh (there was no answer). She is dead at the threshold, her hands on the doorway (v27 — as if still reaching for protection she never received). His matter-of-fact loading of the body onto the donkey and journeying home is presented without comment — the narrator trusts the reader to feel the horror he does not narrate.
19.29: wayyāḇōʾ ʾel-bêtô wayyiqqaḥ ʾet-hammaʾăkelet wayyaḥăzēq bəpilagšô waynattəḥêhā laʿăṣāmêhā lišnêm ʿāśār nətāḥîm wayyəšallāḥêhā bəkol gəḇûl yiśrāʾēl — 'When he entered his house, he took a knife, seized his concubine, and cut her into twelve pieces, limb by limb, and sent her throughout all the territory of Israel.' maʾăkelet (H3979, knife/slaughtering knife — from ʾākal to eat; used for the sacrificial knife Abraham took to Moriah, Gen 22:6,10) — the same instrument of ritual sacrifice becomes an instrument of political summons. nātaḥ (H5408, to cut up/divide in pieces — the word for dismembering a sacrificial animal, Lev 1:6; Exod 29:17) — the cutting is described with the vocabulary of sacrifice. The twelve pieces correspond to the twelve tribes — a territorial summons, sending to every boundary (gəḇûl) of Israel. The act mirrors the cutting of the covenant animal (Gen 15:10,17; Jer 34:18-19) — broken covenant requires a response.
19.30: wayhî kol-hārōʾeh wəʾāmar lōʾ-nihtəyətāh wəlōʾ nirʾətāh kāzōʾt lemibbennê yiśrāʾēl miyyôm ʿălôt bənê-yiśrāʾēl mēʾereṣ miṣrayim ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh śîmû-lākem ʿālêhā ʿūṣû ûḏabbērû — 'Everyone who saw it said: Nothing like this has happened or been seen from the day the sons of Israel came up from the land of Egypt until this day. Set your heart on it, take counsel, and speak.' lōʾ-nihtəyəṯāh wəlōʾ nirʾəṯāh kāzōʾt (nothing like this has been done or seen) — the hyperbole of moral horror: this is the worst thing that has happened since the Exodus, which is the founding event of the nation's existence. The repetition of nihtəyəṯāh / nirʾəṯāh (happened/seen) emphasizes unprecedented evil. The call to 'set your heart on it, take counsel, and speak' (śîmû... ʿūṣû... ḏabbərû) is a summons to corporate covenant response — what Israel does next will determine whether it can still function as YHWH's covenant people.
